blocks.cap:
Here is the road map we will use for the next two problems. You start at town \(A\) on the left and travel through some of the towns \(B\), \(C\), \(D\), \(E\), \(F\), and \(H\), always moving left to right and never doubling back. There are two roads running from \(A\) to \(B\), and every other link is a single road. Notice the two handy shortcuts as well, a direct road from \(A\) to \(D\) and a direct road from \(D\) to \(H\). Follow the arrows and see where the roads can take you.</cap>
</invoke>

blocks.x: Use the road map shown above. You may travel only from left to right along the roads. There are \(2\) roads from town \(A\) to town \(B\), then \(1\) road from \(B\) to \(D\). There is \(1\) road from \(A\) to \(C\), then \(1\) road from \(C\) to \(D\). There is also \(1\) direct road straight from \(A\) to \(D\), and \(1\) direct road from \(D\) to \(H\) further along. Travelling only left to right, how many routes are there from \(A\) to \(D\)?
hints: One clean multiply will not work here, because the trip from \(A\) to \(D\) can pass through different towns first. Try splitting it into separate piles based on which town you reach \(D\) through. What are the different ways the trip could go?

Make three cases that do not overlap and that cover every route. Case 1 goes through \(B\), case 2 goes through \(C\), and case 3 takes the direct road \(A\) to \(D\). Now count each pile on its own using the multiply move from 10.2.

Through \(B\) you pick \(1\) of \(2\) roads to \(B\) and then the \(1\) road \(B\) to \(D\), so \(2 \times 1 = 2\) routes. Through \(C\) there is \(1 \times 1 = 1\) route. The direct road is \(1\) route. You just have to add the three pile counts.
blocks.sol:
One clean multiply will not work here, because the choices are not the same all the way across. So let us split the routes into a few piles based on which town the trip reaches \(D\) through, count each pile on its own, and add at the end. That move is casework, and the rule we keep coming back to is that inside each case we multiply and across the cases we add.

Our three cases never overlap, since a route either passes through \(B\), or through \(C\), or takes the direct road, and never two of those at once. They also cover everything, since every route to \(D\) must do one of those three things.

Case 1, through \(B\). You pick \(1\) of the \(2\) roads from \(A\) to \(B\), then the \(1\) road from \(B\) to \(D\), which gives \(2 \times 1 = 2\) routes.

Case 2, through \(C\). You take the \(1\) road from \(A\) to \(C\), then the \(1\) road from \(C\) to \(D\), which gives \(1 \times 1 = 1\) route.

Case 3, the direct road. There is \(1\) road straight from \(A\) to \(D\), so that is \(1\) route.

Now we add across the cases and you get $$2 + 1 + 1 = 4.$$

So the number of routes from \(A\) to \(D\) is \(\boxed{4}\).

blocks.x: KEY IDEA, counting routes. To count all the ways to make a trip, group the routes by which town in the middle you pass through, count each group on its own, and ADD the groups, which is casework on the route. And when a single route is built from independent stretches that all run through that town, you MULTIPLY the stretch counts, just like 10.2. So multiply along one route to count its group, then add the groups across the towns. That is casework and the Multiplication Principle working side by side.

Look at the road map above. All roads run left to right, and a traveler can only move that way, never back. Between towns \(A\) and \(B\) there are two roads. There is also a direct road straight from \(A\) to \(D\). From \(D\) onward, one road leads \(D\) to \(E\) to \(H\), another leads \(D\) to \(F\) to \(H\), and a third road goes directly from \(D\) to \(H\). Every road on the map eventually feeds into \(D\), so any trip from \(A\) to \(H\) has to pass through \(D\). It turns out there are \(4\) different routes from \(A\) to \(D\). How many routes are there all the way from \(A\) to \(H\)?
hints: The whole trip squeezes through one town. Every route from \(A\) to \(H\) has to pass through \(D\), so split the journey at \(D\) into the first stretch \(A\) to \(D\) and the second stretch \(D\) to \(H\), and handle each pile on its own.

You are told the first stretch \(A\) to \(D\) has \(4\) routes. Now count the second stretch \(D\) to \(H\) by listing its cases. There is \(D\) to \(E\) to \(H\), there is \(D\) to \(F\) to \(H\), and there is the direct road \(D\) to \(H\).

So the second stretch has \(3\) routes. Picking a way from \(A\) to \(D\) and then a way from \(D\) to \(H\) are two independent choices, so this is a multiply, not an add. You just need \(4\times 3\).
blocks.sol:
Every road on the map funnels into \(D\), so a route from \(A\) to \(H\) is really two trips stitched together, first \(A\) to \(D\) and then \(D\) to \(H\). That gives us a clean place to split.
\nThe first stretch is handed to us. There are \(4\) routes from \(A\) to \(D\).
\nFor the second stretch, list the cases from \(D\) to \(H\) and count them honestly. The first case is \(D\) to \(E\) to \(H\). The second case is \(D\) to \(F\) to \(H\). The third case is the direct road \(D\) to \(H\). No two of these are the same route, and there are no other ways forward, so the cases do not overlap and they cover everything. That is \(3\) routes from \(D\) to \(H\).
\nNow comes the spine. Choosing your way from \(A\) to \(D\) and choosing your way from \(D\) to \(H\) are independent, so inside a single trip we multiply, the way we did in 10.2. Each of the \(4\) first-stretch routes can be followed by any of the \(3\) second-stretch routes, which gives $$4\times 3 = 12.$$
\nSo there are \(\boxed{12}\) routes from \(A\) all the way to \(H\).

blocks.x: A three-digit number runs from \(100\) up to \(999\). We want the ones that have exactly one digit equal to \(0\). For example, \(204\) works because it has a single \(0\), and \(190\) works too, but \(500\) has two zeros so it is out, and \(347\) has none so it is out as well. How many three-digit numbers have exactly one digit equal to \(0\)?
hints: The leading digit of a three-digit number can never be \(0\), since a number like \(047\) is really just \(47\). So the lone \(0\) has nowhere to hide in the hundreds spot. That tells you the two places where the single \(0\) could actually sit, and those two spots are a perfect thing to split into cases on.

Make two cases. In the first case the \(0\) sits in the tens place and the units digit is not \(0\). In the second case the \(0\) sits in the units place and the tens digit is not \(0\). Inside each case, figure out how many choices the hundreds digit has and how many choices the remaining free digit has, then multiply those two counts.

Case one gives \(9 \times 9 = 81\), and case two gives \(9 \times 9 = 81\). The two cases never overlap and together they cover every way to have exactly one \(0\). All that is left is to combine the two pile counts.
blocks.sol:
Try counting this with one clean multiply and you hit a snag, because the digit choices are not the same all the way across. The hundreds digit cannot be \(0\), but the tens and units digits can be, and we need exactly one of them to be \(0\). One multiply will not capture that, so we split into a few piles instead and count each on its own. That is casework. Inside each case we multiply the way we did before, and across the cases we add.

First, notice the hundreds digit is never \(0\), since a three-digit number like \(061\) is really just the two-digit number \(61\). So the single \(0\) must live in either the tens place or the units place. That is the cramped part of the problem, so it is exactly what we split on. The two cases never overlap, because the \(0\) cannot be in both spots at once while staying a single \(0\), and together they cover everything.

Case one is the \(0\) in the tens place, with the units digit not \(0\). The hundreds digit can be any of \(1\) through \(9\), which gives \(9\) choices. The tens digit is locked as \(0\). The units digit can be any of \(1\) through \(9\), since it is not allowed to be \(0\), and that gives \(9\) choices. Multiplying inside the case, we get $$9 \times 9 = 81.$$

Case two is the \(0\) in the units place, with the tens digit not \(0\). The hundreds digit again has \(9\) choices, the tens digit can be any of \(1\) through \(9\) for another \(9\) choices, and the units digit is locked as \(0\). Multiplying inside this case, we get $$9 \times 9 = 81.$$

Now we add across the cases, which gives $$81 + 81 = 162.$$

So there are \(\boxed{162}\) three-digit numbers with exactly one digit equal to \(0\).

blocks.x: Look at the whole numbers from \(1\) to \(999\). Some of them have a digit \(2\) somewhere, like \(2\), \(25\), \(112\), or \(200\). How many of the numbers from \(1\) to \(999\) have at least one digit equal to \(2\)?
hints: Counting the numbers that DO have a \(2\) splits into messy overlapping cases, the \(2\) could be in the ones, the tens, or the hundreds place, and some numbers have it in more than one spot. The slick split is just has a \(2\) versus does not have a \(2\). Count the friendlier side, the ones with no \(2\) at all, and subtract from the total.

Picture every number from \(1\) to \(999\) as three digit slots, like \(007\) for \(7\) or \(052\) for \(52\). For a slot to avoid the digit \(2\), it can be any of \(\{0,1,3,4,5,6,7,8,9\}\), which is \(9\) choices. Multiply the slots the 10.2 way to count all three-slot strings with no \(2\).

Three slots with \(9\) choices each gives \(9\times9\times9=729\) strings from \(000\) to \(999\) that avoid the digit \(2\). But \(000\) is really \(0\), which is not in \(1\) to \(999\), so toss it and you have \(728\) numbers with no \(2\). The total count of numbers is \(999\). Now you only need one subtraction.
blocks.sol:
Counting the numbers that have a \(2\) directly is a swarm, since the \(2\) could sit in any of three places and some numbers carry it in two or three places at once. So we use the slick split, has a \(2\) versus does not, and count the lazy side first.

It is much friendlier to count the numbers from \(1\) to \(999\) that have NO digit equal to \(2\), then subtract. Think of every number as three digit slots, so \(7\) is \(007\) and \(52\) is \(052\). For a slot to dodge the digit \(2\), it may be any of \(\{0,1,3,4,5,6,7,8,9\}\), which is \(9\) choices.

Inside this single case we multiply the 10.2 way, three slots with \(9\) choices each, and you get
$$9\times9\times9=729$$
strings from \(000\) to \(999\) that contain no \(2\). One of those strings is \(000\), which stands for \(0\), and \(0\) is not in our range \(1\) to \(999\), so we drop it. That leaves \(729-1=728\) numbers with no digit \(2\).

There are \(999\) numbers in all from \(1\) to \(999\). The two piles, no \(2\) and at least one \(2\), do not overlap and together cover everything, so the at-least-one-\(2\) pile is whatever the no-\(2\) pile leaves behind, which gives
$$999-728=271.$$

So the number of whole numbers from \(1\) to \(999\) with at least one digit equal to \(2\) is \(\boxed{271}\).

blocks.x: KEY IDEA, the complement split. Sometimes the two cleanest cases are simply has the property and does not, and one of those two sides is far easier to count. So count the easy side, then subtract it from the total to get the side you actually wanted. This is still casework, only one case did all the lazy work for you, and the subtracting is the very same total minus the rest you trusted back in 10.1.

You are building a \(3\)-character code. Each character is either a capital letter, with \(26\) choices, or a single digit, with \(10\) choices. The catch is that exactly one of the three characters must be a letter, and the other two must be digits. How many such codes can you make?
hints: A clean single multiply would mix up codes where the letter sits in different spots, so try splitting on something. What is the one thing that changes from code to code here, that you could break into separate piles? Think about WHERE the single letter lands.

Make one case for each position the letter could sit in, so a case for the letter being first, one for it being second, and one for it being third. That is \(3\) cases. Inside any one case, count the letter spot and the two digit spots on their own.

Inside one case the letter spot has \(26\) choices and each digit spot has \(10\), so that single case gives \(26\times10\times10=2600\) codes. Now you have \(3\) cases that each give \(2600\), and they do not overlap. You just need to combine the \(3\) case counts.
blocks.sol:
If you tried to do this in one quick multiply, you would get stuck, because the three spots are not all the same. One spot is a letter and two are digits, but the problem never told you which spot is the letter. So let us split on exactly that snag, which is WHICH position holds the single letter.

The letter can sit in the first spot, or the second spot, or the third spot, and that gives us \(3\) cases. These cases never overlap, since the letter lives in only one place, and together they cover every legal code, so nothing slips through.

Now count inside one case, say the letter is first. The letter spot has \(26\) choices. Each of the other two spots is a digit, so they give \(10\times10=100\) ways. Multiplying inside this one case, you get
$$26\times100=2600.$$

The other two cases work the exact same way, so each of the \(3\) cases gives \(2600\) codes. Inside a case we multiplied, and now across the cases we add, which is the same as
$$3\times2600=7800.$$

So the number of codes is \(\boxed{7800}\).

blocks.x: A three-digit number has a hundreds digit, a tens digit, and a units digit. The hundreds digit cannot be \(0\), or the number would not really have three digits. We want the numbers where all three digits add up to \(6\). For example, \(123\) works because \(1 + 2 + 3 = 6\), and so does \(600\) because \(6 + 0 + 0 = 6\). How many three-digit numbers have digits that add up to \(6\)?
hints: The thing that feels most cramped here is the hundreds digit, since it cannot be \(0\) and it cannot be too big either. What is the largest the hundreds digit could ever be if the three digits only add up to \(6\)? Try splitting into cases based on the hundreds digit.

Call the hundreds digit \(h\). Since \(h \ge 1\) and the other two digits are at least \(0\), the hundreds digit can only be \(1, 2, 3, 4, 5,\) or \(6\). Once you fix \(h\), the tens and units digits just have to add up to \(6 - h\).

For a fixed \(h\), count the ways the tens and units digits can sum to \(6 - h\). If two digits must add up to some number \(n\) (with \(0 \le n \le 9\)), the tens digit can be \(0, 1, \dots, n\) and the units digit is forced, so there are \(n + 1\) ways. That gives \(6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1\) ways for \(h = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\). Now you just need to add those pile counts.
blocks.sol:
The tightest part of this problem is the hundreds digit, so that is the smart thing to split on. The hundreds digit \(h\) has to be at least \(1\), and the three digits together only reach \(6\), so \(h\) can be \(1, 2, 3, 4, 5,\) or \(6\) and no more. That gives us six tidy cases.

Inside each case, once we pin down \(h\), the tens digit and the units digit just have to add up to whatever is left, which is \(6 - h\). When two digits must sum to some value \(n\) between \(0\) and \(9\), the tens digit can be anything from \(0\) up to \(n\) and the units digit is then forced, so there are \(n + 1\) ways.

Now we count each pile on its own.

For \(h = 1\), the rest must sum to \(5\), which gives \(5 + 1 = 6\) ways.
For \(h = 2\), the rest must sum to \(4\), which gives \(4 + 1 = 5\) ways.
For \(h = 3\), the rest must sum to \(3\), which gives \(3 + 1 = 4\) ways.
For \(h = 4\), the rest must sum to \(2\), which gives \(2 + 1 = 3\) ways.
For \(h = 5\), the rest must sum to \(1\), which gives \(1 + 1 = 2\) ways.
For \(h = 6\), the rest must sum to \(0\), which gives \(0 + 1 = 1\) way.

These six cases never overlap, because each number has exactly one hundreds digit, and together they cover everything, because the hundreds digit has to be one of those six values. So we add the pile counts across the cases, the way we add in \(10.1\), after multiplying inside each case, the way we do in \(10.2\), and you get
$$6 + 5 + 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 21.$$

So there are \(\boxed{21}\) three-digit numbers whose digits add up to \(6\).

blocks.x: Splitting a problem into cases and checking every single one is not just a school trick, it has cracked famous problems too. For a long time mapmakers noticed that you never seemed to need more than \(4\) colors to shade any map so that touching countries differ, but nobody could prove it. In \(1976\) two mathematicians finally did, and the way they won was pure casework. They boiled every possible map down to a finite pile of basic shapes, just under \(2{,}000\) of them, and then had a computer grind through the cases one by one to show each one behaved. It was the first big theorem ever settled this way, and it works because the cases never overlap and together they cover every map there is, the same two promises you are leaning on right now.

KEY IDEA, the casework habit. When one clean multiply will not do, pick a smart thing to split on, going after the most cramped part of the problem. Make sure your cases never overlap and together cover everything, so nothing is counted twice and nothing slips through. Then count each case on its own, multiplying inside a case the way you did in 10.2, and add the case counts at the end the way you did in 10.1. That is the whole move. Inside a case you multiply, across the cases you add. In 10.4 Counting Pairs we count how many ways to choose a pair or a group when the order does not matter, so picking Sam then Maya is the same choice as picking Maya then Sam.

review.x: An ice cream stand sells two kinds of sundaes, small and large. A small sundae comes in one of \(5\) flavors. A large sundae comes in one of \(5\) flavors and one of \(2\) sauces. How many different sundaes can you order in all?
hints: The two sizes are not built the same way, so one clean multiply will not work. Split your count into cases by size, the small sundaes in one pile and the large sundaes in another.
Count each pile on its own. For a small sundae you only pick a flavor, so that is \(5\). For a large sundae you pick a flavor and a sauce, so multiply inside that case.
You should now have \(5\) small sundaes and \(5\times2=10\) large sundaes. All that is left is to add the two pile counts across the cases.
review.sol:
A small sundae is built differently from a large one, so trying to multiply straight across does not fit. Let us split what we are counting into two cases by size, and the two sizes never overlap and together cover every sundae, so the split is honest.

Case 1, small sundaes. Here you only choose a flavor, and there are \(5\) flavors, which gives \(5\) small sundaes.

Case 2, large sundaes. Here you choose a flavor and a sauce, so we multiply inside the case and you get \(5\times2=10\) large sundaes.

Now add across the cases, so $$5+10=15.$$

The number of different sundaes is \(\boxed{15}\).

review.x: A toy code is either \(1\) symbol long or \(2\) symbols long. Each symbol is one of \(5\) shapes, and shapes are allowed to repeat. How many possible codes are there?
hints: The codes are not all the same length, so one clean multiply will not catch everything. Try splitting what you are counting by the length of the code, the \(1\)-symbol codes in one pile and the \(2\)-symbol codes in another.
Count each pile on its own. A \(1\)-symbol code has just one slot with \(5\) shapes to choose from. A \(2\)-symbol code has two slots, each with \(5\) shapes, so that pile is \(5 \times 5\).
So the short codes give \(5\) and the long codes give \(5^2 = 25\). Now you have a count for each pile, and there is just one move left.
review.sol:
The codes come in two different lengths, so a single multiply will not do it. This is a job for casework. Split what you are counting by the length of the code, with the \(1\)-symbol codes in one pile and the \(2\)-symbol codes in another. These two piles never overlap, since a code cannot be both lengths at once, and together they cover every code.

Inside each pile we multiply the way we learned before. A \(1\)-symbol code has a single slot with \(5\) shapes to fill it, which gives \(5\) codes. A \(2\)-symbol code has two slots, each with \(5\) shapes and repeats allowed, which gives \(5^2 = 25\) codes.

Now we add across the cases, and you get
$$5 + 5^2 = 5 + 25 = 30.$$
So the number of possible codes is \(\boxed{30}\).

review.x: You write out every whole number from \(1\) to \(99\), one after another. Some of those numbers have one digit and some have two digits. How many digits do you write in total?
hints: One clean multiply will not work here, because the numbers are not all the same length. The one-digit numbers and the two-digit numbers each behave differently. What is the natural thing to split your cases on?
Make two cases. Case one is the numbers \(1\) to \(9\), which each have one digit. Case two is the numbers \(10\) to \(99\), which each have two digits. Now count how many numbers live in each case.
Case one has \(9\) numbers with \(1\) digit each, so that pile is \(9\). Case two has \(90\) numbers with \(2\) digits each, so that pile is \(90\times2=180\). You have both pile counts now.
review.sol:
If you try to count this with one multiply, you get stuck right away, because the numbers do not all have the same number of digits. A \(7\) takes one digit but a \(53\) takes two, so there is no single length to multiply by. That is the snag that tells you to split into cases.

The natural split is by how many digits a number has, so make two piles.

Pile one is the numbers \(1\) to \(9\). There are \(9\) of them and each one has a single digit, which gives \(9\) digits in this pile.

Pile two is the numbers \(10\) to \(99\). There are \(90\) of them and each one has two digits, so that pile is \(90\times2=180\) digits.

The two piles never overlap, since a number cannot be both one digit and two digits, and together they cover every number from \(1\) to \(99\), so nothing slips through. Now add the pile counts and you get
$$9+180=189.$$
So the total number of digits written is \(\boxed{189}\).

review.x: Using pennies, nickels, and dimes, in how many ways can you make \(30\) cents? You can use any number of each coin, including none. How many ways are there in all?
hints: One clean multiply will not work here, because once you pick some coins the rest are not free to be anything. So split into cases. A good thing to split on is the most demanding coin, the dimes, since each dime is worth a lot and there are only a few you could possibly use.

Try each number of dimes on its own. With \(0\) dimes you still have \(30\) cents to fill with nickels and pennies, with \(1\) dime you have \(20\) cents left, with \(2\) dimes you have \(10\) cents left, and with \(3\) dimes you have \(0\) cents left. In each case, once you choose how many nickels you use, the pennies are forced, so just count the allowed numbers of nickels.

Count the nickel choices in each case. With \(30\) cents to fill you can use \(0\) to \(6\) nickels, which is \(7\) ways. With \(20\) cents you can use \(0\) to \(4\) nickels, which is \(5\) ways. With \(10\) cents you can use \(0\) to \(2\) nickels, which is \(3\) ways. With \(0\) cents you can use \(0\) nickels, which is \(1\) way. Now you just need to put these pile counts together.
review.sol:
A single multiply breaks here, because the coins do not have the same freedom all the way across. Once you commit to some dimes, the amount left over changes how many nickels you are even allowed to use. So we split into piles and count each pile on its own.

We will split on the number of dimes, since dimes are the most demanding coin and you can fit only a few of them into \(30\) cents. The possible counts are \(0\), \(1\), \(2\), and \(3\) dimes. These cases never overlap, and together they cover every way, so nothing gets counted twice and nothing slips through.

Here is the quiet trick inside each case. Once we have chosen the dimes and then the nickels, the pennies are completely forced, since the pennies just fill whatever is left. So in each case we only have to count the allowed numbers of nickels.

With \(0\) dimes there is \(30\) cents left to cover, so we can use \(0\) up to \(6\) nickels, which is \(7\) ways.

With \(1\) dime there is \(20\) cents left, so we can use \(0\) up to \(4\) nickels, which is \(5\) ways.

With \(2\) dimes there is \(10\) cents left, so we can use \(0\) up to \(2\) nickels, which is \(3\) ways.

With \(3\) dimes there is \(0\) cents left, so we can use \(0\) nickels, which is \(1\) way.

Now we add the pile counts across the cases, and you get $$7 + 5 + 3 + 1 = 16.$$

So the number of ways is \(\boxed{16}\).

review.x: How many ordered pairs of positive integers \((a,b)\) satisfy \(a^2 + b < 15\)? Here \(a\) and \(b\) are both positive integers, so each is at least \(1\), and the order matters, so \((a,b)\) counts as its own pair.
hints: Try counting in one clean multiply and watch it snag. The trouble is that \(a^2\) grows fast, so the room left for \(b\) shrinks as \(a\) climbs. That uneven shrinking is your cue to split into cases by the value of \(a\).

Lock in which \(a\) values are even possible, then count \(b\) inside each one. Since \(b\) is at least \(1\), you need \(a^2 < 14\), which only allows \(a = 1, 2, 3\). For each, ask how many positive \(b\) keep \(a^2 + b < 15\).

For \(a = 1\) you need \(b < 14\), so \(b\) runs \(1\) to \(13\). For \(a = 2\) you need \(b < 11\), so \(b\) runs \(1\) to \(10\). For \(a = 3\) you need \(b < 6\), so \(b\) runs \(1\) to \(5\). Now you just add the three pile counts.
review.sol:
If you reach for one tidy multiply here, it snags right away, because the choices for \(b\) are not the same all the way across. As \(a\) grows, \(a^2\) eats up more room and leaves less space for \(b\). So we split into cases by the value of \(a\) and count each pile on its own.

First, which \(a\) are even allowed? Since \(b\) is at least \(1\), we need \(a^2 + 1 < 15\), which means \(a^2 < 14\), so \(a\) can only be \(1\), \(2\), or \(3\).

Case \(a = 1\). We need \(1 + b < 15\), so \(b < 14\), and \(b\) runs from \(1\) to \(13\), which gives \(13\) pairs.

Case \(a = 2\). We need \(4 + b < 15\), so \(b < 11\), and \(b\) runs from \(1\) to \(10\), which gives \(10\) pairs.

Case \(a = 3\). We need \(9 + b < 15\), so \(b < 6\), and \(b\) runs from \(1\) to \(5\), which gives \(5\) pairs.

These three cases never overlap, since each fixes a different \(a\), and together they cover every allowed \(a\), so nothing slips through. Inside each case we counted, and across the cases we add, so $$13 + 10 + 5 = 28.$$

\(\boxed{28}\)

review.x: How many two-digit numbers have a units digit that is greater than the tens digit? A two-digit number runs from \(10\) to \(99\), so its tens digit can be \(1\) through \(9\) and its units digit can be \(0\) through \(9\). Count the ones where the units digit is strictly bigger than the tens digit.
hints: The trouble is that the units digit does not have the same number of choices every time, since how big it can be depends on the tens digit. So split into cases on the tens digit \(t\) and count each pile on its own.
Take a tens digit \(t\). The units digit has to be larger than \(t\), so it can be any value from \(t+1\) up to \(9\). That is \(9 - t\) choices for that one case.
Now run \(t\) from \(1\) to \(8\) and write down the pile counts. You get \(8\) when \(t=1\), then \(7\), then \(6\), all the way down to \(1\) when \(t=8\). Add those pile counts.
review.sol:
Here one clean multiply will not work, because the units digit does not get the same number of choices every time. How big it can be depends on the tens digit. So let us split into cases on the tens digit \(t\) and count each pile by itself.

Pick a tens digit \(t\). The units digit must be strictly larger than \(t\), so it can be anything from \(t+1\) up to \(9\). Counting those gives \(9 - t\) choices.

Now walk \(t\) through its values. If \(t = 1\), the units digit can be \(2\) through \(9\), which is \(8\) choices. If \(t = 2\), it can be \(3\) through \(9\), which is \(7\) choices. This keeps shrinking by one, all the way down to \(t = 8\), where the units digit can only be \(9\), so \(1\) choice. Once \(t = 9\) there is nothing bigger left, so that pile is empty and we stop.

The cases never overlap, since each number has just one tens digit, and together they cover every two-digit number we care about. So we add the pile counts $$8 + 7 + 6 + 5 + 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 36.$$

So there are \(\boxed{36}\) two-digit numbers whose units digit is greater than its tens digit.

review.x: A three-digit number runs from \(100\) to \(999\), so its hundreds digit is one of \(1\) through \(9\) and its tens and units digits are each one of \(0\) through \(9\). How many three-digit numbers have a hundreds digit that is at least two times the tens digit?
hints: The hundreds digit has to keep up with two times the tens digit, so the tens digit is the cramped, demanding part. Split into cases on the value of the tens digit \(t\), and notice the units digit never matters, it is free with \(10\) choices no matter what.

In each case you need the hundreds digit \(h\) to satisfy \(h \ge 2t\) with \(h\) from \(1\) to \(9\). When \(t=0\) you need \(h \ge 0\), so all \(9\) hundreds digits work. When \(t=1\) you need \(h \ge 2\), so \(8\) work. Keep going for \(t=2,3,4\), and check when \(2t\) shoots past \(9\) so no hundreds digit can reach it.

Counting the hundreds choices case by case gives \(9\) for \(t=0\), then \(8\), then \(6\), then \(4\), then \(2\), and once \(t \ge 5\) you need \(h \ge 10\) which is impossible, so those cases give \(0\). Add the hundreds choices, then remember each number still has \(10\) free units digits to multiply in.
review.sol:
One clean multiply will not work here, because the hundreds digit choices depend on the tens digit, so the choices are not the same all the way across. That is the signal to split into cases. The tens digit is the demanding part, so we case on it.

First notice the units digit is along for the ride. The condition only ties the hundreds digit to the tens digit, so the units digit is always free with \(10\) choices. We can count the good hundreds-tens pairs and multiply by \(10\) at the very end.

Now case on the tens digit \(t\). In each case we need the hundreds digit \(h\) from \(1\) to \(9\) with \(h \ge 2t\).

When \(t=0\), we need \(h \ge 0\), so all \(9\) hundreds digits work.

When \(t=1\), we need \(h \ge 2\), which leaves \(h\) from \(2\) to \(9\), so \(8\) work.

When \(t=2\), we need \(h \ge 4\), which leaves \(h\) from \(4\) to \(9\), so \(6\) work.

When \(t=3\), we need \(h \ge 6\), which leaves \(h\) from \(6\) to \(9\), so \(4\) work.

When \(t=4\), we need \(h \ge 8\), which leaves \(h\) from \(8\) to \(9\), so \(2\) work.

When \(t \ge 5\), we need \(h \ge 10\), and no single digit reaches \(10\), so none of these cases give anything.

These cases never overlap, since each one fixes a different tens digit, and together they cover every tens digit \(0\) through \(9\), so nothing is counted twice and nothing slips through. Inside each case we multiply, and across the cases we add. Adding the hundreds choices gives \(9+8+6+4+2 = 29\), and each of those hundreds-tens pairs still has \(10\) free units digits, which gives

$$(9+8+6+4+2)\times 10 = 29 \times 10 = 290.$$

So there are \(\boxed{290}\) such three-digit numbers.

review.x: A three-digit number has a hundreds digit, a tens digit, and a units digit. The hundreds digit runs from \(1\) to \(9\), and the tens and units digits each run from \(0\) to \(9\). How many three-digit numbers have their hundreds digit equal to the sum of their tens digit and their units digit?
hints: The hundreds digit \(h\) is doing all the work here, since it has to match \(\text{tens}+\text{units}\). Try splitting into cases on the value of \(h\), one case for each \(h\) from \(1\) to \(9\).
Pick a case, say the hundreds digit is some fixed \(h\). Now you just need the tens and units digits, each from \(0\) to \(9\), to add up to \(h\). Count how many such pairs there are for that \(h\).

For a fixed \(h\), the tens digit can be \(0,1,2,\dots,h\) and the units digit is forced to be \(h\) minus that, so there are \(h+1\) pairs. Now write down \(h+1\) for each \(h\) from \(1\) to \(9\) and you are one step from the total.
review.sol:
The hundreds digit is the demanding part, because it has to equal \(\text{tens}+\text{units}\). So that is the thing to split on. We make one case for each possible hundreds digit \(h\), where \(h\) runs from \(1\) to \(9\).

Inside a single case the hundreds digit is locked at some value \(h\). All we need now is for the tens digit and the units digit, each from \(0\) to \(9\), to add up to \(h\). The tens digit can be any of \(0, 1, 2, \dots, h\), and once we choose it the units digit is forced to be whatever is left over. That gives \(h+1\) ways inside the case.

So the count for each hundreds digit is \(h+1\). When \(h=1\) we get \(2\) ways, when \(h=2\) we get \(3\) ways, and so on up to \(h=9\), which gives \(10\) ways. The cases never overlap, since each number has just one hundreds digit, and together they cover every number we want.

Now we add across the cases, which gives
$$2 + 3 + 4 + \cdots + 10 = 54.$$

Inside each case we multiplied (well, we counted the forced pairs), and across the cases we added, and the whole thing falls apart cleanly once we split on the hundreds digit. So the answer is \(\boxed{54}\).

review.x: There are two boxes on a table. Box one holds \(8\) cards and box two holds \(6\) cards. You first pick one of the two boxes, and then from that box you draw \(2\) cards one after the other, without putting the first one back. The order you draw them in matters, so drawing card A then card B counts as different from drawing card B then card A. How many different ordered draws are possible in all?
hints: The two boxes have different numbers of cards, so a single multiply for the whole problem will not work. Split into cases by which box you reached into, and count each box on its own.
Take box one first. It has \(8\) cards, so the first card has \(8\) choices and the second has \(7\) left, which gives \(8\times 7\). Then do box two the same way with its \(6\) cards.
You now have the draws from box one and the draws from box two. These are two separate piles that never share a draw, so to finish you add the two pile counts together.
review.sol:
The catch here is that the two boxes do not hold the same number of cards, so one clean multiply across the whole problem would give the wrong count. The rescue is casework. Split by which box you picked, count each box on its own, and add the piles at the end.

Inside a case you multiply, just like in 10.2. For box one there are \(8\) cards, so the first draw has \(8\) choices and the second has \(7\) left since you do not put the first one back, which gives \(8\times 7=56\) ordered draws.

For box two there are \(6\) cards, so the first draw has \(6\) choices and the second has \(5\) left, which gives \(6\times 5=30\) ordered draws.

These two piles never overlap, because a draw from box one can never also be a draw from box two, and together they cover every possible draw. So across the cases you add, just like in 10.1, and you get $$56+30=86.$$ So the number of ordered draws is \(\boxed{86}\).

review.x: A four-digit number runs from \(1000\) to \(9999\), so its first digit is never \(0\). We want the four-digit numbers that have exactly one digit equal to \(0\), meaning one of the four digits is a \(0\) and the other three are all nonzero. How many four-digit numbers have exactly one digit equal to \(0\)?
hints: The leading digit can never be \(0\), so the lone \(0\) can only sit in one of the other three positions. That is a clean thing to split on. Try making one case for each spot the \(0\) could land in.

Split into cases by which position holds the \(0\). There are \(3\) positions it could be (the hundreds, the tens, or the ones), so you get \(3\) cases. In each case, the thousands digit is one of \(1\) through \(9\), so \(9\) choices, the \(0\) is locked into its chosen spot, and the two leftover spots must each be nonzero, so \(9\) choices each.

Each case has the same size, namely \(9\) for the thousands digit times \(9\) times \(9\) for the two leftover nonzero spots, which is \(9 \times 9 \times 9\) per case. Now multiply that by the \(3\) cases.
review.sol:
Here is the snag if you reach for one clean multiply. The four spots do not all behave the same way, because the rule "exactly one \(0\)" depends on where that \(0\) lands, and the leading spot cannot even hold a \(0\). So instead of one multiply, we split the count into a few cases, count each case on its own, and add the case counts at the end. That move is casework.

What should we split on? Go after the cramped part. The thousands digit is never \(0\), so the single \(0\) is forced to sit in one of the other three positions, which are the hundreds, the tens, and the ones. That gives us \(3\) tidy cases, one for each spot the lone \(0\) could take.

Now count one case, say the \(0\) sits in the hundreds spot. The thousands digit can be any of \(1\) through \(9\), so \(9\) choices. The hundreds spot is locked as \(0\), so \(1\) choice. The tens and ones must each be nonzero so we keep exactly one \(0\), and that is \(9\) choices each. Inside this case we multiply, the way we did in 10.2, and you get $$9 \times 1 \times 9 \times 9 = 9 \times 9 \times 9 = 729.$$

The other two cases, with the \(0\) in the tens spot or in the ones spot, work out exactly the same way, since the thousands digit still has \(9\) choices and the two leftover nonzero spots still have \(9\) choices each. So each of the \(3\) cases gives \(729\).

The cases never overlap, because a number lands in only one case depending on which single spot its \(0\) occupies, and together they cover every four-digit number with exactly one \(0\). So across the cases we add, the way we did in 10.1, which is the same as multiplying the per-case count by \(3\), and you get $$3 \times 729 = 2187,$$ or all at once $$3 \times 9 \times 9 \times 9 = 2187.$$

So there are \(\boxed{2187}\) four-digit numbers with exactly one digit equal to \(0\).

review.x: Count the whole numbers from \(1\) to \(500\) that have at least one digit equal to \(7\). How many such numbers are there?
hints: Trying to count the numbers that *have* a \(7\) means worrying about the \(7\) being in the ones place, or the tens place, or the hundreds place, and those piles overlap. Flip it around. It is friendlier to count the numbers with *no* \(7\) at all, then subtract that pile from \(500\).

Split the \(500\) numbers by how many digits they have. There are the small ones from \(1\) to \(99\), then the three-digit ones from \(100\) to \(499\), and finally \(500\) sitting by itself. In each pile, count how many numbers dodge the digit \(7\) completely.

Think of every number from \(000\) to \(499\) as three slots. The hundreds slot can be \(0,1,2,3,4\) with no \(7\) to avoid, which is \(5\) choices. The tens and ones slots each run \(0\) through \(9\) but must skip the \(7\), which leaves \(9\) choices each. Multiply those to count the no-\(7\) numbers, remember \(000\) is not really in our range, and check whether \(500\) has a \(7\).
review.sol:
Counting the numbers that *have* a \(7\) gets tangled fast, because the \(7\) could hide in the ones place or the tens place or the hundreds place, and a number like \(77\) would get counted more than once. So we play the lazy card. We count the numbers with *no* \(7\) anywhere, and then subtract that pile from \(500\). This is casework where one side did almost all the work for us.

To count the no-\(7\) numbers cleanly, picture every number from \(0\) to \(499\) as three digit slots, a hundreds slot, a tens slot, and a ones slot. The hundreds slot can be \(0,1,2,3,\) or \(4\), and none of those is a \(7\), so that is \(5\) choices. The tens slot runs from \(0\) to \(9\) but must avoid the \(7\), which leaves \(9\) choices. The ones slot is the same, \(9\) choices. Inside this one case we multiply, the way we always do, and we get
$$5 \times 9 \times 9 = 405.$$

That count of \(405\) secretly includes \(000\), which is just \(0\) and is not in our range of \(1\) to \(500\), so it does not steal anything from us, because \(0\) has no \(7\) either way and we will handle the endpoints honestly. What it covers is the whole stretch from \(1\) to \(499\), plus the stand-in \(0\). The number \(500\) is the only one left out of the slot picture, and \(500\) has no \(7\) in it, so it belongs in the no-\(7\) pile too.

So the no-\(7\) numbers from \(1\) to \(500\) are the \(405\) from the slots, minus the \(1\) for the \(0\) that is not really in our range, plus the \(1\) for \(500\), which gives
$$405 - 1 + 1 = 405.$$

There are \(405\) numbers with no \(7\) at all. Everything else must have at least one \(7\), so we subtract from the full \(500\) and get
$$500 - 405 = 95.$$

\(\boxed{95}\)

review.x: A code has \(3\) characters. Each character is either a capital letter, and there are \(26\) of those, or a digit, and there are \(10\) of those. The catch is that exactly one of the three characters is a digit, so the other two must both be letters. How many such codes are there?
hints: One clean multiply will not work here, because the single digit could sit in any of the three spots. Try splitting into cases on WHICH position holds the lone digit.
There are \(3\) cases, one for each position the digit could take. In every case, that one spot is a digit, so it has \(10\) choices, and the other two spots are letters.
Inside each case you multiply, and you get \(10\times 26\times 26\) codes. Now you have \(3\) cases that all give the same count, so think about how to combine them.
review.sol:
If you try to multiply straight across, you get stuck, because each spot is not "any of \(36\)" all the way through. The rule that exactly one spot is a digit means the spots do not all behave the same, so one clean multiply would miss it. The fix is to split into cases on WHICH position holds the single digit.

There are exactly \(3\) places that lone digit could live, the first spot, the second spot, or the third spot. That is our \(3\) cases.

Take one case, say the digit sits in the first spot. That first spot is a digit, so it has \(10\) choices. The other two spots must be letters, and each letter spot has \(26\) choices, which gives $$26\times 26 = 676.$$ So this one case has $$10\times 676 = 6760$$ codes.

The other two cases, digit in the second spot and digit in the third spot, work the same way and each give \(6760\) codes too. Notice the two promises hold. No code is counted twice, because each code has its digit in exactly one position, and the cases cover everything, because the digit has to be somewhere.

Inside each case we multiplied, and across the \(3\) cases we add, which gives $$3\times 10\times 676 = 20280.$$

So there are \(\boxed{20280}\) such codes.

review.x: How many three-digit numbers have digits that add up to \(4\)? In a three-digit number the hundreds digit is at least \(1\), and the three digits together must sum to \(4\).
hints: The hundreds digit cannot be \(0\), and it cannot be more than \(4\) or the digits would already add past \(4\). So split into cases on the hundreds digit \(h\), where \(h\) runs from \(1\) to \(4\).

Once you fix the hundreds digit \(h\), the tens and units digits must add up to \(4-h\). For a target sum \(s\) the number of ways to pick the tens digit (with the units digit forced) is \(s+1\). So \(h=1\) needs tens+units \(=3\), \(h=2\) needs \(=2\), \(h=3\) needs \(=1\), and \(h=4\) needs \(=0\).
Count each case on its own. \(h=1\) gives \(4\) ways, \(h=2\) gives \(3\) ways, \(h=3\) gives \(2\) ways, \(h=4\) gives \(1\) way. Now add the four pile counts.
review.sol:
The slot-and-box trick stalls here because the digits do not all have the same number of choices, so we split into cases. The cleanest thing to split on is the hundreds digit \(h\), since it is the most cramped (it has to be at least \(1\), and it cannot exceed \(4\) or the sum already overshoots). So \(h\) runs from \(1\) to \(4\).

Inside each case the hundreds digit is fixed, so the tens and units digits must add up to whatever is left, which is \(4-h\). If those two digits must add up to \(s\), you can pick the tens digit anywhere from \(0\) up to \(s\) and the units digit is forced, which gives \(s+1\) ways.

Now count each pile.

When \(h=1\), the rest must sum to \(3\), giving \(3+1=4\) ways.
When \(h=2\), the rest must sum to \(2\), giving \(2+1=3\) ways.
When \(h=3\), the rest must sum to \(1\), giving \(1+1=2\) ways.
When \(h=4\), the rest must sum to \(0\), giving \(0+1=1\) way.

These cases never overlap, since each fixes a different hundreds digit, and together they cover every possibility. So we add the pile counts across the cases, which gives $$4+3+2+1=10.$$

So there are \(\boxed{10}\) three-digit numbers whose digits add up to \(4\).

review.x: We want to count ordered pairs of positive integers \((a, b)\) that satisfy \(a + b^2 < 20\). Both \(a\) and \(b\) have to be at least \(1\). How many such pairs \((a, b)\) are there?
hints: One clean multiply will not work here, because \(b\) and \(b^2\) change how much room is left for \(a\). Try splitting into cases based on the value of \(b\).

For each \(b\), the rule \(a + b^2 < 20\) becomes \(a < 20 - b^2\). So \(b=1\) needs \(a < 19\), \(b=2\) needs \(a < 16\), \(b=3\) needs \(a < 11\), and \(b=4\) needs \(a < 4\). Once \(b=5\) we have \(b^2 = 25\), which is already too big, so the cases stop.

Now count the positive whole values of \(a\) in each case. With \(a < 19\) you get \(18\) values, with \(a < 16\) you get \(15\), with \(a < 11\) you get \(10\), and with \(a < 4\) you get \(3\). All that is left is to add these four pile counts together.
review.sol:
If we try to count everything in one multiply, we get stuck, because the \(b^2\) part eats up a different amount of room depending on \(b\). So let us split into cases on the value of \(b\) and count each pile on its own.

For any \(b\), the rule \(a + b^2 < 20\) means \(a < 20 - b^2\), and we just need the positive whole values of \(a\) that fit.

When \(b = 1\) we need \(a < 19\), which gives \(18\) choices for \(a\).

When \(b = 2\) we need \(a < 16\), which gives \(15\) choices.

When \(b = 3\) we need \(a < 11\), which gives \(10\) choices.

When \(b = 4\) we need \(a < 4\), which gives \(3\) choices.

When \(b = 5\) we already have \(b^2 = 25\), which is too big on its own, so there is nothing left to count and the cases stop here.

These four cases never overlap, since each fixes a different \(b\), and together they cover every pair. So we add the pile counts and you get $$18 + 15 + 10 + 3 = 46.$$

So the number of pairs is \(\boxed{46}\).

blocks.x: In Lesson 10.2 we lined up a row of choices and multiplied straight across, and that counted every outcome where the order mattered, like first place and second place in a race. But a lot of real questions are not like that. Think about a handshake between two people. If you shake my hand, that is the same single handshake as me shaking yours, so you and me is not a different handshake from me and you. When order stops mattering like this, a plain multiply quietly counts everything twice, once for each way of saying the same thing. The good news is that the overcount is gentle and tidy, so once we spot it we can undo it with one small fix, and that little fix is the whole heart of this lesson.
Six friends meet up, and each friend shakes hands exactly once with every other friend. No two friends shake hands more than once. How many handshakes happen in all?
hints: A handshake is a PAIR of friends, and the pair Ana with Ben is the very same handshake as Ben with Ana, so the order does not matter. And everyone comes from the one group of six friends, not from two separate groups. So this is a pair count, where you will want to watch out for counting each handshake twice.

Set up the count the easy way first. Each of the \(6\) friends reaches out to the other \(5\) friends, so multiply to get \(6 \times 5\). Now ask yourself the key question for pairs, did that count each handshake once, or did it sneak each one in twice?
You have \(6 \times 5 = 30\), but every handshake got counted twice, once from each friend in the pair. So the honest count is half of \(30\), and all that is left is to take that half.
blocks.sol:
A handshake is really just an unordered pair of friends, where Ana shaking Ben's hand is the same single handshake as Ben shaking Ana's hand. So this is a pair count, and everyone comes from the one group of six friends.
\nLet us count the easy way first and fix the trouble after. Each of the \(6\) friends shakes hands with the other \(5\) friends, and multiplying those gives $$6 \times 5 = 30.$$
\nBut look closely, because that \(30\) counts every handshake twice. When Ana reaches out to Ben we count it once, and when Ben reaches out to Ana we count the same handshake again. Each pair got tallied from both sides, so the count is doubled.
\nThe honest answer is half as big, so we divide by \(2\) to undo the overcount, which gives $$30 / 2 = 15.$$
\nSo the six friends share \(\boxed{15}\) handshakes.

blocks.x: KEY IDEA, counting pairs. To count pairs where the order does not matter, first count the ordered way, the way you already trust from the multiplication principle. You have \(n\) choices for the first pick, then \(n-1\) choices left for the second, which gives \(n(n-1)\). But every pair just got counted twice, once as A then B and once as B then A, which is the same handshake both times. So you cut the overcount in half and divide by \(2\). The number of pairs you can make from \(n\) things is \(\frac{n(n-1)}{2}\). Count the ordered way, then divide by \(2\) to undo the double count.

<svg viewBox="0 0 416 304" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="A four by four grid showing why we divide by two when counting pairs. The four people A, B, C, D label both the columns across the top and the rows down the left side. Each cell is a person paired with another. The four cells along the main diagonal, where a person meets themselves, are blacked out and counted as not real pairs. The six cells above the diagonal are shaded gold and the six cells below the diagonal are shaded blue. These two triangles are mirror copies of each other, so the pair A and B appears once in the gold triangle and once again as B and A in the blue triangle. That makes 12 shaded cells, which are the ordered pairs. Each real pair is counted twice, once in each triangle, so 12 divided by 2 gives 6 actual pairs.">

blocks.cap: Here are \(4\) people, A, B, C, D, written across the top and down the side. Every cell pairs the person in its row with the person in its column. The \(4\) black cells run down the middle, where someone would meet themselves, so those are not real pairs and we toss them out. That leaves the gold triangle above with \(6\) cells and the blue triangle below with \(6\) cells, which is \(12\) cells in all. Notice the two triangles are mirror copies. The pair A with B is a gold cell, and B with A is the matching blue cell, so each real pair shows up twice. To undo that double counting we share the \(12\) equally between the two copies, and you get \(12 \div 2 = 6\) actual pairs.

blocks.x: A tennis class has \(5\) girls. A doubles team is any group of \(2\) of them. How many different doubles teams can be formed?
hints: First ask what kind of count this is. A team is just \(2\) girls picked from the class, and the order you name them in does not matter, since pairing Ana with Beth is the same team as pairing Beth with Ana. And notice you are choosing both girls from one single group, not one girl from one group and one from another. So this is counting pairs.

Set it up like a handshake. Each of the \(5\) girls could partner with any of the other \(4\), which gives \(5 \times 4\) ways if order mattered. But order does not matter here, so each team got counted twice, once from each girl's side. That tells you what to do next with that product.
You have \(5 \times 4 = 20\), and since every team was counted twice you divide by \(2\). So you just need \(\frac{20}{2}\).
blocks.sol:
A doubles team is just \(2\) girls chosen from the class, and the order does not matter, since the team made of Ana and Beth is the very same team as Beth and Ana. So this is a pair count, and both girls come from one single group.
\nStart by letting each girl reach out to a partner. Any of the \(5\) girls can pair with any of the other \(4\), and that gives $$5 \times 4 = 20.$$
\nBut that \(20\) counts every team twice, once when Ana picks Beth and once when Beth picks Ana, and those are the same team. So the honest count is half as big, and you divide by \(2\), which gives $$\frac{5 \times 4}{2} = \frac{20}{2} = 10.$$
\nSo you can form \(\boxed{10}\) different doubles teams.

blocks.x: In a round-robin tournament, every player plays every other player exactly once. Picture \(8\) players signing up, and think about who faces whom. Each match brings together two of the players, and no two players meet more than once. How many matches are played in this \(8\)-person round-robin tournament?
hints: Think about what one match really is. It is a pair of two players, and the match where Ana plays Ben is the very same match as the one where Ben plays Ana, so order does not matter. And notice that both players come from the one group of \(8\), not from two separate groups.

Each of the \(8\) players could face any of the other \(7\), so multiply to get \(8 \times 7\). Now ask the key question, since both players come from the same group, does counting this way count each match once or twice, and if it is twice then you will need to divide by \(2\).
You have \(8 \times 7 = 56\), and every match got counted twice because each pair shows up from both players' points of view. So take half of \(56\) and you are there.
blocks.sol: Every match is just a pair of players, so we are choosing \(2\) players from the \(8\), where order does not matter since Ana playing Ben is the same match as Ben playing Ana. Start with the multiplying habit. Each of the \(8\) players can face any of the other \(7\), which gives \(8 \times 7 = 56\). But that counts each match twice, once from each player's side, so we undo the overcount by dividing by \(2\). That gives $$\frac{8 \times 7}{2} = \frac{56}{2} = 28.$$ Since both players come from the same group of \(8\), the divide-by-\(2\) is exactly what keeps us honest, and we land on \(\boxed{28}\) matches.

blocks.x: Here is the one to keep. Choosing \(2\) things from \(n\) is the workhorse of this whole chapter, and it always counts the same way. A handshake in a room, a match in a round-robin, a team of two, a pair of co-presidents, every one of them is just a pair pulled from \(n\), and every one of them comes out to \(\frac{n(n-1)}{2}\). A round-robin where everyone plays everyone exactly once is this and nothing more. When you meet a new pair problem, draw two boxes for the two picks, multiply to fill them, then halve because the two boxes are really the same pair seen from each side.

A club has \(12\) members, and it needs to pick \(2\) of them to serve together as co-presidents. The two co-presidents share the exact same job, so there is no "first" president and no "second" president, just a pair of people doing the role side by side. How many different pairs of co-presidents are possible?
hints: Start by asking what kind of count this is. Are you choosing a pair where the order does not matter, or are you making two separate picks from two groups you can tell apart? Here both co-presidents come from the one club and they share the same role, so this is a pair where order does not matter.

Lean on the multiplying habit. The first co-president can be any of the \(12\) members, and the second can be any of the remaining \(11\), which gives \(12 \times 11\). Now decide the key thing, whether picking Ana then Ben is different from picking Ben then Ana. Since they share one role, those are the same pair, so you will need to divide by \(2\).
You have \(\frac{12 \times 11}{2}\). Work out \(12 \times 11 = 132\), then take half of \(132\).
blocks.sol: Both co-presidents come from the same club, so this is one group, and the two of them share the same role, which means the pair is unordered. Reach for the multiplying habit first. The first co-president could be any of the \(12\) members, and the second could be any of the \(11\) who are left, and you get \(12 \times 11\). But watch the trap. Choosing Ana and then Ben gives the very same pair of co-presidents as choosing Ben and then Ana, so every pair has been counted twice. The honest count is half as big, so you divide by \(2\), which gives $$\frac{12 \times 11}{2} = \frac{132}{2} = 66.$$ So there are \(\boxed{66}\) different pairs of co-presidents.

blocks.x: An ice cream shop has \(11\) flavors. A double scoop uses \(2\) different flavors, and the order they are stacked does not matter. How many different double scoops are possible?
hints: First decide what kind of count this is. You are choosing \(2\) flavors from one single list of \(11\), and a scoop of chocolate on vanilla is the very same scoop as vanilla on chocolate. So this is a pair where the order does not matter, and both flavors come from the same group, not two separate groups.

Set it up like a handshake. The first flavor has \(11\) choices and the second has \(10\) left, so a first pass gives \(11 \times 10\). But picking chocolate then vanilla and picking vanilla then chocolate land on the same double scoop, so each scoop got counted twice. That tells you whether to divide by \(2\) here.
You have \(\frac{11 \times 10}{2}\) to finish. Work out the top first, then take half of it, and you have your count.
blocks.sol:
A double scoop is just an unordered pair of flavors, because a scoop of mint on top of strawberry is the exact same dessert as strawberry on top of mint. So this is the handshake idea wearing a new costume, and we count pairs from one group of \(11\).

Start the way we multiply in 10.2. The first flavor can be any of the \(11\), and once it is chosen the second flavor can be any of the \(10\) that are left, and you get $$11 \times 10 = 110.$$

But watch the same trap as the handshakes. Choosing chocolate first and vanilla second gives the same double scoop as choosing vanilla first and chocolate second, so every scoop has been counted twice. To undo that overcount we take half, which gives $$\frac{11 \times 10}{2} = \frac{110}{2} = 55.$$

So there are \(\boxed{55}\) different double scoops.

blocks.x: Seven points are marked on a page, and no three of them lie in a straight line. Someone draws a straight segment joining every pair of points. How many segments are drawn in all?
hints: Each segment just joins two of the points, so this is a pair where the order does not matter. Ben-and-Ana is the same segment as Ana-and-Ben. And all seven points come from one single group, not two separate groups, so think about whether you might be counting each segment twice.

Set up the count. Each of the \(7\) points can reach out to the other \(6\) points, which gives \(7 \times 6\). But since the segment from one point to another is the very same segment going back, every pair got counted twice, so you will need to divide by \(2\).
You have \(7 \times 6 = 42\), and now you just halve it. Take \(\frac{42}{2}\) and you are done.
blocks.sol:
Every segment is really just a pair of points, since a segment is fixed the moment you pick the two points it joins. So counting segments is the same as counting how many pairs you can make from the seven points.

Start by letting each point reach out to all the others. Each of the \(7\) points can connect to the other \(6\), which gives \(7 \times 6 = 42\). But watch the trap here, because the segment from one point to another is the exact same segment as the one going back the other way. That means every pair got counted twice. To fix the overcount you take half, so you divide by \(2\).

That gives $$\frac{7 \times 6}{2} = \frac{42}{2} = 21.$$

So the number of segments drawn is \(\boxed{21}\).

blocks.x: Here is the contrast worth carrying everywhere. When the two things you pick come from the same group, like two people in one room, every pair gets counted twice, so you divide by \(2\). But when you pick one thing from one group and one from a different group, like a leader and a follower or a team from each of two schools, the two picks can never swap places, so nothing is ever counted twice. There you just multiply the group sizes and you do not divide at all.

  <circle cx="348" cy="150" r="15" fill="var(--soft)" stroke="var(--slate)" stroke-width="2"/>
  <circle cx="348" cy="210" r="15" fill="var(--soft)" stroke="var(--slate)" stroke-width="2"/>

  <text x="86" y="28" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="15" font-weight="600" fill="var(--ink)" text-anchor="middle">4 squares</text>
  <text x="348" y="48" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="15" font-weight="600" fill="var(--ink)" text-anchor="middle">3 circles</text>

  <text x="220" y="282" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="17" font-weight="700" fill="var(--gold-deep)" text-anchor="middle">4 × 3 = 12 connections</text>
</svg>
blocks.cap: Here are two separate groups, a left group of \(4\) squares and a right group of \(3\) circles. Reach out from the top square and you can draw a line to each of the \(3\) circles, which is \(3\) connections from that one square. Every one of the \(4\) squares does the same thing, so you stack up \(4\) groups of \(3\) and you get \(4 \times 3 = 12\) lines in all. Notice that no line repeats. A square is never a circle, so the two sides never swap places, and that is why you pick one from each group, multiply, and do not divide. The total stays \(\boxed{12}\).

blocks.x: A dance class has \(7\) leaders and \(6\) followers. Every leader dances exactly once with every follower. How many leader-and-follower dances take place?
hints: Before you count anything, ask what kind of pairing this is. A dance joins one leader with one follower. Are those two coming from the same group, where order would not matter, or are they coming from two different groups you can tell apart?

You are pulling one pick from the leaders and one pick from the followers, two separate groups, so multiply their sizes. Then pause and decide whether you need to divide by \(2\) the way you do when both picks come from one group.
Multiplying gives \(7\times6\), and since a leader and a follower can never trade roles, no dance gets counted twice, so there is no dividing by \(2\) here. Just finish that one product.
blocks.sol:
Each dance pairs one leader with one follower, and those two picks come from TWO different groups, the leaders and the followers. When the picks come from groups you can tell apart, you simply multiply the sizes, so $$7\times6=42.$$

Notice there is no dividing by \(2\) this time. We divide by \(2\) only when both picks come from the same group, because then choosing Ana then Ben is the same pair as choosing Ben then Ana. Here a leader and a follower can never swap roles, so no dance is ever counted twice and nothing needs undoing.

The number of dances is \(\boxed{42}\).

blocks.x: At a party there are \(5\) women and \(4\) men. Every woman shakes hands with every other person at the party, both the other women and all the men. The men, though, never shake hands with each other, so no handshake happens between two men. Each handshake that does happen happens just once. How many handshakes take place in all?
hints: A handshake is just a pair of people, and the order does not matter, since you shaking my hand is the same single handshake as me shaking yours. So really you are counting how many allowed pairs there are. Notice the pairs come in different kinds, some join two women, some join a woman and a man, and some would join two men.

Split the count into kinds of pairs. For two women, both come from the same group of \(5\), so that is a pair where you reach out and then divide by \(2\). For a woman and a man, the two picks come from different groups, so you just multiply the sizes and do not divide. For two men, none are allowed, so that kind adds nothing.
You should have woman-with-woman giving \(\frac{5 \times 4}{2}\), woman-with-man giving \(5 \times 4\), and man-with-man giving \(0\). Work each one out, then add the three counts together.
blocks.sol:
A handshake is a pair of people, and order does not matter, so all we are really doing is counting the allowed pairs. The trick is that the pairs come in different kinds, so let us split them up and count each kind on its own.
\nFirst the woman-with-woman handshakes. Both people in such a pair come from the same group of \(5\) women. Each woman could reach out to the other \(4\), and you get \(5 \times 4\), but that counts Ana-with-Bea and Bea-with-Ana as two different things when they are the very same handshake. So we divide by \(2\) to undo that double count, which gives \(\frac{5 \times 4}{2} = 10\).
\nNext the woman-with-man handshakes. Now the two people come from groups you can tell apart, one woman and one man, so nothing gets counted twice and there is no dividing by \(2\). You simply multiply the sizes, which gives \(5 \times 4 = 20\).
\nLast the man-with-man handshakes. The rule says no two men shake hands, so this kind adds nothing at all, a flat \(0\).
\nNow add the kinds together, and you get \(10 + 20 = 30\).

$$\boxed{30}$$

blocks.x: KEY IDEA, pairs the casework way (a callback to 10.3). When some pairs are off limits, you do not have to count them one by one. Count every pair with \(\frac{n(n-1)}{2}\), then take away the forbidden ones, the way the diagonals of a polygon are just all the pairs of corners with the \(n\) sides removed. Or split the pairs into kinds, like same-group versus cross-group, count each kind on its own, and add. That is the casework from 10.3 wearing a new hat, only now the things you are splitting and subtracting are pairs.

blocks.cap: Here are all \(6\) people at the gathering, four women as filled dots and two men as open dots. Draw a line for every pair and you get \(\frac{6 \times 5}{2} = 15\) lines in all. But the two men will not shake hands with each other, so that single man-with-man line is crossed out, which is \(1\) pair to remove. The allowed handshakes are \(15 - 1 = 14\). Count every pair, then subtract the pairs that are not allowed.

blocks.x: A hexagon is a flat shape with \(6\) corners and \(6\) sides. A diagonal is a straight line that joins two corners which are not already joined by a side. Picture standing at one corner and drawing a line to every other corner, then doing the same from each corner, and being careful not to count any of the sides as diagonals. How many diagonals does the hexagon have?
hints: Every line in this picture joins two corners, so you are choosing a pair of corners where the order does not matter. All \(6\) corners come from one single hexagon, not two separate groups, so this is a pairs question and not a multiply-the-two-sizes question.

First count how many pairs of corners there are at all. Each of the \(6\) corners reaches out to the other \(5\), which gives \(6 \times 5\), and then you divide by \(2\) because joining corner A to corner B is the very same line as joining B to A.

Once you know there are \(15\) pairs of corners in all, remember that some of those lines are sides, not diagonals. The hexagon has \(6\) sides, so take those away from the \(15\) and you are left with just the diagonals.
blocks.sol:
Start with a friendly idea. Every pair of corners in the hexagon is joined by a line, and that line is either a side or a diagonal, never both. So if you count all the pairs of corners first, the diagonals are just what is left after you take the sides away.

Count the pairs of corners. Each of the \(6\) corners can reach out to the other \(5\) corners, and that gives \(6 \times 5\). But joining corner A to corner B is the same line as joining corner B to A, so every pair got counted twice. Divide by \(2\) to undo that double count, which gives $$\frac{6 \times 5}{2} = 15.$$

So there are \(15\) pairs of corners in all. Of these, \(6\) of the lines are the sides of the hexagon, and a side is not a diagonal. Take those away and the diagonals are $$15 - 6 = 9.$$

So the hexagon has \(\boxed{9}\) diagonals.

blocks.x: Here is the move worth keeping. Pick any two corners of a polygon and the line joining them is either a side or a diagonal, with nothing in between, so the diagonals are just all the corner pairs after you take away the ones that are sides. There are \(\frac{n(n-1)}{2}\) pairs of corners in all, and exactly \(n\) of them are sides, one for each edge of the shape, which leaves \(\frac{n(n-1)}{2} - n\) diagonals. Notice the trick. You did not hunt for diagonals one by one, you counted every pair the easy way and then subtracted the few you did not want, and that habit of building what you need by removing what you do not is going to keep paying off.

<svg viewBox="0 0 440 300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="A square with its four corners marked as dots. The four sides of the square are drawn in slate blue and the two diagonals that cross inside are drawn in orange. To the right, three lines of math explain that the number of all corner pairs is 4 times 3 over 2 equals 6, that 4 of those pairs are sides, and that 6 minus 4 leaves 2 diagonals.">

  <text x="70" y="275" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="13" fill="var(--slate)">4 sides</text>
