  <text x="252" y="240" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="16" font-weight="700" fill="var(--accent)">6 − 4 = 2</text>
</svg>
blocks.cap: Picture a square with its \(4\) corners. Count every pair of corners and you get \(\frac{4 \times 3}{2} = 6\) pairs in all. The slate lines are the \(4\) sides, where each pair of corners that sits next to each other is joined. Take those away and the orange lines are what is left, so the diagonals are \(6 - 4 = 2\). Every pair of corners is either a side or a diagonal.

blocks.x: A decagon is a flat shape with \(10\) corners and \(10\) straight sides. A diagonal is a straight segment that joins two corners but is not one of the sides. How many diagonals does a decagon have?
hints: Every diagonal joins two corners, and the order you name them in does not matter, so this is a pair where Ana to Ben is the same as Ben to Ana. And all the corners live in one single group, so you will count pairs from that one group rather than multiply two different sizes.

Count the pairs of corners first. Each of the \(10\) corners can reach the other \(9\), which gives \(10 \times 9\), and since each pair got counted twice you divide by \(2\). That total includes the sides though, so afterward you will need to take the sides back out.
You should have \(\frac{10 \times 9}{2} = 45\) pairs of corners. Now just remove the \(10\) that are sides and not diagonals.
blocks.sol: Start by counting every pair of corners, since each pair gives one straight segment joining them. There are \(10\) corners, and each one reaches out to the other \(9\), which gives \(10 \times 9\). But Ana to Ben and Ben to Ana are the same segment counted twice, so you divide by \(2\) and you get $$\frac{10 \times 9}{2} = 45.$$ Those \(45\) segments include the \(10\) sides of the decagon, and sides are not diagonals. So take them back out, which gives \(45 - 10 = 35\). The decagon has \(\boxed{35}\) diagonals.

blocks.x: Six people play in a checkers tournament. Every pair of players plays exactly \(3\) games against each other, so each twosome faces off three times. How many games are played in all?
hints: First decide what you are really counting. A game is played by a pair of people, and the pair Ana with Ben is the same matchup as Ben with Ana, so order does not matter here. And the two players come from the same group of six, not two separate groups, which is the clue that this is a pair where you will need to undo a double count.

Count the pairs first. Each of the \(6\) people could face each of the other \(5\), which gives \(6 \times 5\), but that counts every matchup twice since Ana with Ben and Ben with Ana are the same game, so divide by \(2\). Then think about what each pair does after that.
You have found that there are \(\frac{6 \times 5}{2} = 15\) pairs. Each pair plays \(3\) games, so the last step is to multiply \(15\) by \(3\).
blocks.sol: Start by counting the matchups, since each game is played by a pair of players. Each of the \(6\) people reaches out to the other \(5\), which gives \(6 \times 5 = 30\). But that counts every matchup twice, because Ana playing Ben is the very same pairing as Ben playing Ana, so divide by \(2\) to undo the overcount and you get $$\frac{6 \times 5}{2} = 15$$ pairs of players. Now bring in the part that makes this more than a plain pair count. Each pair does not play once, it plays \(3\) games, so multiply the number of pairs by the games per pair, which gives $$15 \times 3 = 45.$$ So the tournament has \(\boxed{45}\) games in all.

blocks.x: KEY IDEA, pairs taken several times. When every pair does the same thing more than once, like a team that plays each other team \(k\) games, you do not start over. Count the pairs first the way you just did, with \(\frac{n(n-1)}{2}\), and then multiply that by \(k\). So pairs first, then the multiplication principle from 10.2 sitting right on top, and you get \(\frac{n(n-1)}{2} \times k\). Choosing the pair handles the order does not matter part, and the \(\times k\) just stacks the repeats on after.

A sports league has \(12\) teams split into two divisions of \(6\). Each team plays every team in its own division twice, and every team in the other division once. How many games are in the full season?
hints: Sort the games into two kinds, the ones inside a division and the ones across the divisions. Inside a division, a game is a PAIR of teams from one group where order does not matter, so Ana versus Ben is the same game as Ben versus Ana. Across the divisions you are picking one team from each of two groups you can tell apart, which is a different kind of count.

For one division, first count the pairs with \(\frac{6 \times 5}{2}\), then remember each pair plays twice, and there are two divisions. For the across games, you do not divide by \(2\), since the two teams come from groups you can tell apart, so just multiply the sizes \(6 \times 6\).
One division gives \(\frac{6 \times 5}{2} = 15\) pairs, doubled is \(30\), and two divisions make \(60\). The across games are \(6 \times 6 = 36\). Now add \(60\) and \(36\).
blocks.sol: Split the season into two kinds of games, the ones inside a division and the ones across the divisions. Start inside one division. A game there is a pair of teams where order does not matter, so the number of pairs is \(\frac{6 \times 5}{2} = 15\). Each of those pairs plays twice, which gives \(15 \times 2 = 30\) games in that one division. There are two divisions just like it, so that part is \(30 \times 2 = 60\). Now the across games. Here you pick one team from one division and one team from the other, and those are two groups you can tell apart, so nothing gets double counted and you never divide by \(2\). Each of the \(6\) teams plays each of the other \(6\) once, which gives \(6 \times 6 = 36\). Add the two kinds together and you get \(60 + 36 = 96\). $$\boxed{96}$$

blocks.x: Four students volunteer for the school store, and exactly \(2\) of them work together each day. No pair of students works together more than once. How many days at most can be scheduled?
hints: Two students working together is a PAIR, and the order does not matter, since Ana with Ben is the same team as Ben with Ana. And notice all four come from one single group of volunteers, not two separate groups.

Each student can be paired with the other \(3\), so reach for the multiplying habit and you get \(4 \times 3\). But every pair gets counted twice that way, so think about whether you should divide by \(2\).
You have \(\frac{4 \times 3}{2}\), which is \(\frac{12}{2}\). The most days is just how many different pairs there are, so finish the division.
blocks.sol:
Each day puts a different pair of students together, and no pair ever repeats, so the most days you can schedule is simply the number of different pairs you can make.

To count the pairs, think of each of the \(4\) students reaching out to the other \(3\), which gives \(4 \times 3 = 12\). But that counts every pair twice, once from each person's side, since Ana with Ben is the same team as Ben with Ana. So the honest count is half as big and you divide by \(2\), which gives $$\frac{4 \times 3}{2} = \frac{12}{2} = 6.$$

There are \(6\) different pairs, so the greatest number of days that can be scheduled is \(\boxed{6}\).

blocks.x: KEY IDEA, pairs are triangular numbers. Counting pairs is secretly just adding up \(1 + 2 + \cdots + (n-1)\). Bring people in one at a time and the second person makes \(1\) new pair, the third makes \(2\), the fourth makes \(3\), and so on, so the running total of new pairs marches up the staircase \(1, 2, 3, \ldots\) until the last person adds \(n-1\). That is why \(\frac{n(n-1)}{2}\) and \(1 + 2 + \cdots + (n-1)\) are the same number wearing two outfits. And you can add a long climbing sum like that in a flash with the Gauss trick, by folding the staircase in half and pairing the ends, first plus last, then second plus second to last, and so on, since every one of those little pairs sums to the same thing.

In a \(17\)-person round-robin, every pair of players meets exactly once, so the number of matches is the number of pairs. There is a neat second way to count those matches by adding instead of using a formula. Line the \(17\) players up in a row and look at each player's matches against the players before them. The \(2\)nd player has \(1\) match against an earlier player, the \(3\)rd player has \(2\) earlier opponents, the \(4\)th has \(3\), and so on, all the way up to the \(17\)th player, who has \(16\) earlier opponents. Adding these up counts every match exactly once and gives the sum \(1 + 2 + 3 + \cdots + 16\). What is the value of this sum, \(1 + 2 + 3 + \cdots + 16\)?
hints: You are adding up the whole numbers from \(1\) all the way to \(16\). This is the same total as the number of pairs you can choose from \(17\) players, since order does not matter and Ana versus Ben is the same match as Ben versus Ana.

You could add straight across, but there is a faster way. Try folding the staircase in half. Pair the smallest term with the largest, then the next smallest with the next largest, and notice that every pair makes the same total.
Pairing the ends gives \(1 + 16 = 17\), \(2 + 15 = 17\), and so on down to \(8 + 9 = 17\). Count how many pairs of \(17\) you have, then multiply.
blocks.sol: This is the triangular-number way of counting pairs. Instead of adding \(1 + 2 + 3 + \cdots + 16\) term by term, fold the staircase in half the way Gauss did and pair the terms from the ends. The smallest plus the largest gives \(1 + 16 = 17\), the next pair gives \(2 + 15 = 17\), and you keep going inward down to \(8 + 9 = 17\). Every pair sums to the same \(17\). There are \(8\) of these pairs, since \(16\) terms split into \(8\) cozy couples, which gives $$8 \times 17 = 136.$$ This matches the pair-counting formula straight away, because choosing a pair from \(17\) players is \(\frac{17 \times 16}{2} = 136\). Two roads, one answer, and the adding road shows you that counting pairs is really just stacking up \(1 + 2 + \cdots + (n-1)\). So the sum is \(\boxed{136}\).

blocks.x: A round-robin tournament has \(15\) teams, and every team plays every other team exactly once. The tournament organizer needs to print a schedule, so she wants to know the total number of matches. How many matches are played in all?
hints: First decide what kind of count this is. A match is just a pair of teams, and the order does not matter, since the match between team A and team B is the very same match as the one between team B and team A. Notice too that both teams come from the one group of \(15\), not from two separate groups, so this really is a counting-pairs problem.

Lean on the multiplying habit. Each of the \(15\) teams reaches out to the other \(14\) teams, which gives \(15 \times 14\). But ask yourself the key question, did you count each match once or twice. Since A versus B and B versus A are the same match, you have counted everything twice, so this time you do need to divide by \(2\).
You are almost there. Work out \(15 \times 14 = 210\), and then take half of that to undo the double counting.
blocks.sol: A match is a pair of teams, and the order inside a pair does not matter, so this is a counting-pairs question where both teams come from the one group of \(15\). Start the way we always do, by letting each team reach out. Every one of the \(15\) teams could play each of the other \(14\) teams, and you get \(15 \times 14 = 210\). But watch the trap. When team A plays team B and when team B plays team A, that is the very same match counted twice, so \(210\) is twice too big. Undo the overcount by dividing by \(2\), which gives $$\frac{15 \times 14}{2} = \frac{210}{2} = 105.$$ So choosing \(2\) teams from \(15\) lands on \(\boxed{105}\) matches.

blocks.x: An icosahedron is a solid with \(20\) triangular faces, \(12\) vertices, and \(30\) edges. An interior diagonal is a segment joining two vertices that do not lie on a common face. How many interior diagonals does an icosahedron have?
hints: A segment just needs two corners, and swapping which corner you name first gives the same segment, so this is a pair where order does not matter, all coming from one group of \(12\) vertices.

Start by counting every pair of vertices, multiply \(12\) by \(11\) and divide by \(2\) since each segment got named twice. Then think about which of those pairs you do not want, the ones that sit on a common face.
All pairs come to \(\frac{12 \times 11}{2} = 66\). Every face is a triangle, so the only pairs sharing a face are the \(30\) edges. Now you just have \(66\) and \(30\) waiting to be combined.
blocks.sol:
A diagonal is a segment, and a segment is just two corners chosen together, where it does not matter which one you name first. So this is a pair count from the one group of \(12\) vertices.

Start by counting all the pairs of vertices you could possibly join. Each of the \(12\) vertices can reach out to the other \(11\), and then you divide by \(2\) so that joining Ana to Ben and joining Ben to Ana count as one segment, which gives $$\frac{12 \times 11}{2} = 66.$$

That \(66\) counts every segment, but we only want the interior ones. So we remove the pairs that lie on a common face. Here is the lovely part. Every face is a triangle, and a triangle has no diagonals of its own, so the only vertex-pairs that share a face are the ones that are actual edges of the solid. There are \(30\) edges, so there are \(30\) pairs to take out.

Taking those out leaves $$66 - 30 = 36.$$

So an icosahedron has \(\boxed{36}\) interior diagonals.

blocks.x: Those pair counts \(1, 3, 6, 10, 15, \ldots\) have a lovely name, the triangular numbers, and the name is not just for show. Set down one dot, then a row of two beneath it, then a row of three, and so on, and the dots really do pile up into a neat triangle, with the number of dots in each triangle matching exactly how many pairs you can choose. It is a quiet little gift that the answer to "how many handshakes" is the very same as "how many dots stack into a triangle," so the next time you count pairs you can picture them settling into that shape.

KEY IDEA, counting pairs. To count the pairs you can make from one group, multiply then divide by 2, which gives \(\frac{n(n-1)}{2}\), since each thing reaches out to the other \(n-1\) and then you undo the double count. Across two groups you can tell apart, just multiply the sizes and never divide, because nothing got counted twice. When some pairs are not allowed, subtract the forbidden ones or split into cases, and to count the diagonals of a polygon take all the pairs of corners and subtract the \(n\) sides, which is \(\frac{n(n-1)}{2} - n\). In 10.5 An Introduction to Probability, these very counts become the way we measure how likely something is, since once you know how many pairs there are you can ask how often one of them shows up.

review.x: A round-robin tournament has \(13\) players, and the rule is simple, every player plays exactly one match against every other player. So each possible pair of players meets exactly once over the whole tournament. How many matches are played in all?
hints: A match is just a pair of players, and the match where Ana plays Ben is the same match as the one where Ben plays Ana, so order does not matter here. And the two players in a match come from one single group of \(13\), not two separate groups.

Each of the \(13\) players faces the other \(12\), which gives \(13 \times 12\). But that counts every match twice, once from each player's side, so think about whether you need to divide by \(2\) to undo the double count.
You have \(13 \times 12 = 156\), and now you just need to cut that double count in half by dividing by \(2\).
review.sol: A match is a pair of players, so we are counting pairs from one group of \(13\). Start the way we always do, by letting each player reach out to everyone else. Each of the \(13\) players faces the other \(12\), and multiplying gives \(13 \times 12 = 156\). But here is the trap, because the match where Ana plays Ben is the very same match as the one where Ben plays Ana, yet we just counted it twice, once from each side. To undo that double count we cut the total in half, so we divide by \(2\) and get $$\frac{13 \times 12}{2} = 78.$$ So the tournament has \(\boxed{78}\) matches.

review.x: Fourteen people meet at a small gathering, and each person shakes hands once with every other person. How many handshakes happen in all?
hints: A handshake between two people is a pair where the order does not make a new handshake, since Ana shaking Ben's hand is the same as Ben shaking Ana's hand. And everyone comes from one single group of \(14\), not two separate groups, so this really is a count of pairs.

Set it up by giving each of the \(14\) people a hand to reach out, so each one reaches the other \(13\), and you get \(14\times13\). Now ask whether that counts every handshake once or twice, and since each handshake got counted from both sides, you will need to divide by \(2\).
You should be looking at \(\frac{14\times13}{2}\). Work out \(14\times13\) first, and then take half of it to land on the answer.
review.sol:
A handshake joins two people, and the order does not matter, since Ana shaking Ben's hand and Ben shaking Ana's hand are the very same handshake. So we are counting pairs, and everyone comes from one group of \(14\).

Give each person a hand to reach out. Each of the \(14\) people reaches the other \(13\), which gives \(14\times13\). But this counts every handshake twice, once from each person's side, so the honest count is half as big and we divide by \(2\), and you get $$\frac{14\times13}{2}=91.$$

So the number of handshakes is \(\boxed{91}\).

review.x: A team of \(16\) players picks \(2\) of themselves to be co-captains. The two co-captains share the job equally, so it does not matter which one you name first. How many different pairs of co-captains are possible?
hints: First decide what kind of count this is. The two co-captains come from one single group, the same team, and naming Maya and then Leo gives the very same pair as naming Leo and then Maya. So order does not matter, which means you are counting pairs.

Reach for the pair-counting habit. Each of the \(16\) players could pair up with any of the other \(15\), which gives \(16 \times 15\). But every pair just got counted twice, once from each player's side, so you will need to divide by \(2\) to undo that double count.
You have \(16 \times 15 = 240\) from the first step. Now cut that in half to fix the overcount, and that single division by \(2\) gives you the answer.
review.sol:
Both co-captains come from the same team, and the two of them share the job, so naming one first and the other second does not make a new choice. That tells us we are counting pairs, where order does not matter.
\nThink about it from one player's view first. Any single player could team up with any of the other \(15\) players, and there are \(16\) players who could do that reaching out, which gives \(16 \times 15 = 240\). But here is the trap. When Maya pairs with Leo and when Leo pairs with Maya, that is the very same pair of co-captains counted twice. So \(240\) is exactly double the honest answer.
\nWe undo that double count by cutting it in half, and you get \(\frac{16 \times 15}{2} = 120\). So there are $$\boxed{120}$$ different pairs of co-captains.

review.x: Eighteen points lie on a circle. A chord is drawn joining each pair of points, so every two points are connected by exactly one chord. How many chords are drawn in all?
hints: A chord is just a segment between two of the points, so really you are choosing a pair of points. Ask yourself whether the order matters here, since the chord from point \(A\) to point \(B\) is the same chord as the one from \(B\) to \(A\), and whether you are picking from one group or two.

Try counting it the multiplying way first. Each of the \(18\) points reaches out to the other \(17\) points, which gives \(18 \times 17\). Now think hard about whether that count sees each chord once or twice, since order does not matter for a pair.
You have \(18 \times 17 = 306\) from the reaching-out count, but every chord got counted from both ends, so you are one divide-by-\(2\) away from the honest total.
review.sol:
A chord just connects two of the points, so drawing a chord is the same as choosing a pair of points. The order does not matter, because the chord joining point \(A\) to point \(B\) is the exact same chord as the one joining \(B\) to \(A\), and all the points sit in one single group on the circle.
\nLet us count the reaching-out way first. Each of the \(18\) points can connect to any of the other \(17\) points, which gives \(18 \times 17 = 306\). But watch the trap. When point \(A\) reaches over to \(B\) and when \(B\) reaches back to \(A\), that is one and the same chord counted twice. So \(306\) is exactly double the real number of chords.
\nWe undo the overcount by dividing by \(2\), which gives
$$\frac{18 \times 17}{2} = \frac{306}{2} = 153.$$
\nSo the number of chords drawn is \(\boxed{153}\).

review.x: A pentagon is a shape with \(5\) corners and \(5\) sides. A diagonal is a straight segment that joins two corners but is not one of the sides. How many diagonals does a pentagon have?
hints: A diagonal joins two corners, and a corner paired with another corner is the same no matter which one you name first, so this is a count of pairs where order does not matter, all coming from one group of \(5\) corners.

Start by counting every pair of corners with \(\frac{5 \times 4}{2}\), since each corner reaches the other \(4\) and you divide by \(2\) so you do not count the same pair twice. Then remember some of those pairs are the \(5\) sides, not diagonals.
You have \(\frac{5 \times 4}{2} = 10\) corner pairs in all. Now take away the \(5\) that are sides and finish the subtraction.
review.sol: Start by counting every pair of corners, because each segment of the pentagon, side or diagonal, joins two corners. There are \(5\) corners, and each one can pair with the other \(4\), which gives \(5 \times 4\). But that counts every pair twice, once from each corner, like corner Ana to corner Ben and corner Ben to corner Ana being the same segment, so you divide by \(2\) and you get $$\frac{5 \times 4}{2} = 10$$ corner pairs in all. Now \(10\) is too many, because some of those pairs are the sides of the pentagon, not diagonals. A pentagon has \(5\) sides, so take those out, which gives \(10 - 5 = 5\). So the pentagon has \(\boxed{5}\) diagonals.

review.x: A stop sign is a regular octagon, a flat shape with \(8\) straight sides and \(8\) corners. A diagonal is a straight segment joining two corners that are not already next to each other along an edge. How many diagonals does an octagon have?
hints: Every segment you can draw connects two corners, and a segment from corner A to corner B is the same segment as the one from B to A. So this is a question about pairs of corners where order does not matter, all chosen from the single group of \(8\) corners. Start by asking how many pairs of corners there are in all.

Count all the pairs first. Each of the \(8\) corners can reach the other \(7\), and you get \(8 \times 7\), but that counts every pair twice, once from each end, so divide by \(2\). Then remember that some of those segments are sides of the octagon, not diagonals, and think about how many you should take away.
You found \(\frac{8 \times 7}{2} = 28\) corner pairs in total. An octagon has \(8\) sides, and each side is one of those pairs but is not a diagonal, so just subtract those \(8\) from the \(28\).
review.sol: Every segment between two corners is decided by a pair of corners, and the pair from A to B is the very same pair as from B to A, so order does not matter and all the corners come from one group of \(8\). First count how many pairs of corners there are in all. Each of the \(8\) corners reaches out to the other \(7\), which gives \(8 \times 7\), but that counts each pair twice, once from each end, so you divide by \(2\) and get $$\frac{8 \times 7}{2} = 28.$$ That \(28\) is every segment you could draw between two corners. But \(8\) of those segments are the sides of the octagon, and a side is not a diagonal, so take those away, which gives \(28 - 8 = 20\). So an octagon has \(\boxed{20}\) diagonals.

review.x: A chess match is held between two schools. One school has \(7\) players and the other school has \(8\) players. Every player plays each player from the other school exactly once. How many games are played?
hints: Ask first whether this is a pair where order does not matter. Here each game needs one player from one school and one player from the other school, so the two players come from two groups you can tell apart, not from a single group.

Set up the count by multiplying the size of one school by the size of the other, since each of the \(7\) players faces each of the \(8\) players. Then ask whether you need to divide by \(2\), and notice that the two players come from different groups so nothing gets counted twice.
You are almost there, so just work out \(7 \times 8\) and that is your total with no dividing by \(2\).
review.sol:
Every game pairs one player from the first school with one player from the second school. Take any one of the \(7\) players on the first team, and that player has \(8\) opponents to face on the other team, which gives \(8\) games. The same is true for each of the \(7\) players, so you multiply, \(7 \times 8 = 56\).

Now check whether you have overcounted. When two players come from the same group you would count each handshake twice and need to divide by \(2\), but here the two players come from different schools that you can tell apart, so no game is ever counted twice and you never divide by \(2\). The honest total is $$7 \times 8 = 56,$$ so the number of games is \(\boxed{56}\).

review.x: At a small gathering there are \(6\) women and \(3\) men. Every woman shakes hands once with every other person in the room, women and men alike. The men, though, never shake hands with each other, so no handshake happens between two men. How many handshakes happen in all?
hints: Some of these handshakes are between two women, and some are between a woman and a man. Ask yourself which kind is a pair where order does not matter, both picked from the same group, and which kind reaches across two different groups. The two kinds get counted in different ways.

For the woman-with-woman handshakes you are choosing \(2\) from the same \(6\) people, so multiply \(6 \times 5\) and then divide by \(2\) since shaking is the same handshake counted twice. For the woman-with-man handshakes the two people come from groups you can tell apart, so you just multiply \(6 \times 3\) and do not divide by \(2\) at all.
Work out \(\frac{6 \times 5}{2}\) for the women together, then \(6 \times 3\) for the women with the men, and all that is left is to add those two counts.
review.sol: Let us sort the handshakes into the kinds that happen. First the women among themselves. A handshake here is a pair of women, where order does not matter, since Ana shaking Bea is the very same handshake as Bea shaking Ana. Each of the \(6\) women reaches out to the other \(5\), which gives \(6 \times 5\), but that counts every handshake twice, so we halve it and get \(\frac{6 \times 5}{2} = 15\). Next the women with the men. Here the two people come from groups we can tell apart, a woman from one side and a man from the other, so nothing is double counted and we simply multiply. That is \(6 \times 3 = 18\), with no dividing by \(2\). The men never shake each other, so there is nothing more to add. Putting the two counts together gives \(15 + 18 = 33\). So the number of handshakes is \(\boxed{33}\).

review.x: A small club has \(6\) players, and every player plays every other player \(4\) times. How many games are played in all?
hints: Start by thinking about who is in a single game. A game is just two players together, and the game between Ana and Ben is the same game as the one between Ben and Ana, so order does not matter. Everyone comes from the one group of \(6\), so this is a pair-counting question, not a pick-from-two-different-groups question.

Count the pairs of players first. Each of the \(6\) players reaches out to the other \(5\), which gives \(6 \times 5\), but that counts each pair twice, once from each side, so divide by \(2\). Once you know how many pairs there are, remember that each pair does not play just once.
You should have found \(\frac{6 \times 5}{2} = 15\) pairs of players. Every one of those pairs plays \(4\) games, so all that is left is to multiply \(15\) by \(4\).
review.sol:
First figure out how many different pairs of players there are, since each pair is one matchup. Each of the \(6\) players reaches out to the other \(5\), and you get \(6 \times 5\). But when Ana faces Ben and when Ben faces Ana that is the very same pair counted twice, so the honest count is half as big, which means we divide by \(2\) and you get $$\frac{6 \times 5}{2} = 15$$ pairs of players.

Now each of those \(15\) pairs plays \(4\) games, not just one. So we multiply the number of pairs by the games each pair plays, which gives \(15 \times 4 = 60\).

So the total number of games is \(\boxed{60}\).

review.x: A sports league has \(8\) teams split into two divisions of \(4\) teams each. Every team plays each of the other teams in its own division twice, and plays each team in the other division once. How many games are in the whole season?
hints: Sort the games into two kinds before you count, the games inside a division and the games across the two divisions. Inside one division a game is just a pair of teams and the order does not matter, so playing the Hawks against the Owls is the same matchup as the Owls against the Hawks. A game across divisions picks one team from each separate group, so think about whether that is a pair from one group or one team from each of two groups.

Inside a single division of \(4\) teams, count how many pairs of teams there are with \(\frac{4 \times 3}{2}\), then double it because each pair meets twice. Do that for both divisions. For the across games, you pick one team from one division and one from the other, two separate groups you can tell apart, so you just multiply the sizes and never divide by \(2\).

One division has \(\frac{4 \times 3}{2} = 6\) pairs, and \(6 \times 2 = 12\) games, so the two divisions together give \(12 + 12 = 24\) inside games. The across games give \(4 \times 4 = 16\). Now you just add the inside total and the across total.
review.sol:
There are really two different kinds of games here, so let us count each kind on its own and add them at the end.
\nFirst the games inside a division. A game between two teams in the same division is just a pair of teams, and the order does not matter, since playing team A against team B is the very same game as team B against team A. With \(4\) teams in a division, the number of pairs is
$$\frac{4 \times 3}{2} = 6.$$\nEach of those pairs meets twice, so one division has \(6 \times 2 = 12\) games. There are two divisions built the same way, so the inside games total
$$12 + 12 = 24.$$
\nNow the games across the divisions. Here you pick one team from the first division and one team from the second division. These come from two groups you can tell apart, so nothing gets double counted and you do not divide by \(2\), you simply multiply the sizes and you get
$$4 \times 4 = 16.$$
\nThe inside games and the across games never overlap, so we add them and you get
$$24 + 16 = 40.$$
\nSo the season has \(\boxed{40}\) games.

review.x: Find the sum of the first \(14\) even numbers, \(2 + 4 + 6 + \cdots + 28\). What is the value of this sum?
hints: This is really a pairing question, the same trick that turns counting handshakes into something easy. The numbers run from \(2\) up to \(28\), and you can match them up two at a time. Try teaming the smallest with the largest and see what each pair adds up to.

Pair the ends inward, so \(2\) goes with \(28\), then \(4\) goes with \(26\), and keep walking inward. Every pair adds up to the same amount, so figure out that common sum and then count how many pairs you can make from the \(14\) numbers.
Each pair adds to \(30\), and \(14\) numbers split into \(7\) pairs. All that is left is to work out \(7 \times 30\).
review.sol: Instead of adding the numbers one slow step at a time, let us pair them from the ends, the same folding move that made counting pairs so friendly. Walk in from both sides at once. The smallest and the largest team up first, and you get \(2 + 28 = 30\). The next ones in are \(4\) and \(26\), and you get \(4 + 26 = 30\) again. Keep going, \(6 + 24 = 30\), and the same total keeps coming up.\nEvery pair lands on \(30\), which is the nice part. There are \(14\) numbers in the list, and folding them in half makes \(7\) pairs. So you have \(7\) pairs that each add to \(30\), which gives $$7 \times 30 = 210.$$\nThat is exactly the Gauss staircase trick. You can also see it as \(14 \times 15 = 210\), since the average of the two ends times the count gives the same total. So the sum is \(\boxed{210}\).

review.x: A cube has \(8\) vertices, \(12\) edges, and \(6\) square faces. An interior diagonal is a line joining two vertices that do not share a face, so it cuts straight through the inside of the cube instead of running along an edge or across a face. How many interior diagonals does a cube have?
hints: An interior diagonal joins two corners, and swapping which corner you start from gives the very same diagonal, so this is a pair where order does not matter. Every corner comes from the one set of \(8\) vertices, so you are counting pairs from a single group, not picking from two separate groups.

Start by counting all the pairs of corners you could join. There are \(8\) vertices, so the number of pairs is \(\frac{8 \times 7}{2}\). Not all of those pairs are interior diagonals though, since some joined corners are edges and some are diagonals lying flat on a face.

From all the pairs, take away the ones that are not interior. There are \(12\) edges to remove, and each of the \(6\) square faces has \(2\) face diagonals, so subtract \(6 \times 2\) of those too. Whatever pairs are left must be the interior diagonals.
review.sol: Start by joining corners with no rules at all. Any line that joins two of the cube's corners comes from choosing a pair of vertices, and since starting at one corner or the other gives the same line, order does not matter. With \(8\) vertices the number of pairs is $$\frac{8 \times 7}{2} = 28.$$ So there are \(28\) lines joining corners in total. Now we sift out the ones that are not interior. Some of those pairs are edges of the cube, and a cube has \(12\) edges, so we take those away. Some other pairs run straight across a face, and each square face has \(2\) face diagonals, so across the \(6\) faces that is \(6 \times 2 = 12\) face diagonals to take away as well. What is left after removing the edges and the face diagonals must be the lines that pierce the inside, which gives $$28 - 12 - 12 = 4.$$ So a cube has \(\boxed{4}\) interior diagonals.

review.x: A pizza shop offers \(20\) different toppings, and a 2-topping pizza uses two different toppings where the order you name them does not matter. How many different 2-topping pizzas can be made?
hints: First decide what kind of count this is. You are choosing two toppings from one single list of \(20\), and a mushroom-and-onion pizza is the same as an onion-and-mushroom pizza, so the order does not matter. That makes this a count of pairs from one group, not two separate groups.

Pair counting starts the same way as the handshakes did. Each of the \(20\) toppings could pair with the other \(19\), which gives \(20 \times 19\). But naming topping A first then B gives the same pizza as B first then A, so every pizza got counted twice. To undo that overcount you divide by \(2\).
Set it up as \(\frac{20 \times 19}{2}\). Now \(20 \times 19 = 380\), and all that is left is to take half of \(380\).
review.sol: Picking two different toppings from one list of \(20\), where order does not matter, is exactly a count of pairs. Think of it the way we counted handshakes. Each of the \(20\) toppings can join with any of the other \(19\), which gives \(20 \times 19\) ordered picks. But choosing mushroom then onion makes the very same pizza as onion then mushroom, so every pizza has been counted twice. To honestly count each pizza once you divide by \(2\), and you get $$\frac{20 \times 19}{2} = \frac{380}{2} = 190.$$ So there are \(\boxed{190}\) different 2-topping pizzas.

review.x: A flat seven sided sign is cut in the shape of a heptagon, with seven corners around its edge. A designer wants to draw every diagonal, meaning every straight line that joins two corners but is not one of the seven sides. How many diagonals does the heptagon have?
hints: Joining two corners with a line treats the two corners the same way, since the line from corner A to corner B is the very same line from corner B to corner A. So this is a pair where order does not matter, and all seven corners come from the one heptagon, not from two separate groups.

Count every pair of corners first, using \(\frac{n(n-1)}{2}\) with \(n = 7\). You multiply \(7 \times 6\) and divide by \(2\) since each pair would otherwise be counted twice. That gives all the corner-joining lines, both the sides and the diagonals together.
You have \(21\) corner pairs in all, but \(7\) of those lines are the sides of the heptagon, not diagonals. Take those away, so find \(21 - 7\).
review.sol: A diagonal joins two corners, and the line from one corner to another is the same line no matter which corner you name first, so every diagonal is really a pair of corners where order does not matter. That is exactly a pair-counting job. With \(7\) corners, the number of ways to choose a pair is \(\frac{7 \times 6}{2} = 21\), where you multiply because each of the \(7\) corners reaches out to the other \(6\), and you divide by \(2\) because that count sees each pair twice. So there are \(21\) lines joining corners in all. But not every such line is a diagonal. The \(7\) sides of the heptagon also join two corners, and we do not want to count those. So we take all the corner pairs and subtract the sides, which gives \(21 - 7 = 14\). The heptagon has \(\boxed{14}\) diagonals.

blocks.x: You just spent a whole chapter learning to count carefully, and now you get to spend it on something fun. We are going to use counting to measure how likely something is. Picture flipping a fair coin. Do it once and you cannot say much, but flip it again and again, a hundred times, a thousand times, and about half of those flips come up heads. That steady half is the clue, so we say the probability of heads is \(\frac{1}{2}\). That is really all a probability is, the fraction of the time we expect an outcome to show up over many, many tries. And here is the happy part. In the clean cases we care about, we do not have to flip anything forever to find that fraction. We can just count.

Here is the whole idea in one line. When every outcome of an experiment is equally likely, the probability of an event is the number of favorable outcomes divided by the total number of outcomes, $$P = \frac{\text{favorable}}{\text{total}}.$$ So probability is really counting wearing a new coat. You count the outcomes you want on top, you count all the outcomes on the bottom, and both of those counts come straight from the tools you just spent this chapter building.

A fair \(6\)-sided die has faces numbered \(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\), and each face is equally likely to land face up. You give the die one roll. What is the probability of rolling a \(3\)? Give your answer as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: Every roll lands on one of the faces \(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\), and since the die is fair they are all equally likely. How many faces are there in all? That total goes on the bottom of your fraction.
Now look for the rolls that count as a win. You want to land on a \(3\), so ask how many of the six faces show a \(3\). That favorable count goes on the top of your fraction.
You have \(1\) favorable face out of \(6\) equally likely faces, so write that as a fraction and check it is already in lowest terms.
blocks.sol: Start by listing what can happen on one roll. The die can land on \(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\), and because it is fair, every one of those is equally likely. So the total count is \(6\). Next count the rolls that win. You are hoping for a \(3\), and exactly one face shows a \(3\), so the favorable count is \(1\). When every outcome is equally likely the probability is the favorable count over the total count, which gives $$\frac{1}{6}.$$ That fraction is already in lowest terms, so the probability of rolling a \(3\) is \(\frac{1}{6}\).

blocks.x: A fair \(6\)-sided die is rolled once, and every face from \(1\) to \(6\) has the same chance of landing on top. You win if the number that shows is odd. Find the probability that the number rolled is odd. Give your answer as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: Start by listing the equally likely outcomes. A fair die can land on \(1, 2, 3, 4, 5,\) or \(6\), so how many faces are there in all?
Now look at which of those faces count as a win. The odd numbers are \(1, 3, 5\), so how many of the \(6\) faces are favorable?
You have \(3\) favorable faces out of \(6\) total, which gives \(\frac{3}{6}\). All that is left is to write that fraction in lowest terms.
blocks.sol: A fair die has \(6\) equally likely faces, so the total count is \(6\). The odd faces are \(1, 3, 5\), so \(3\) of the \(6\) equally likely faces are favorable. Putting the favorable count over the total count and you get $$\frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}.$$ So the probability of rolling an odd number is \(\frac{1}{2}\).

blocks.x: A fair \(6\)-sided die has the numbers \(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\) on its faces, and every face is equally likely to land on top. A perfect square is a number you get by multiplying a whole number by itself, like \(1 = 1 \times 1\) and \(4 = 2 \times 2\). Find the probability that the number you roll is a perfect square. Give your answer as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: First pin down what is equally likely. Since the die is fair, each of the faces \(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\) has the same chance, so the total count is \(6\). That total goes on the bottom of your fraction.
Now find the favorable faces. Go through \(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\) and ask of each one whether it is a perfect square, meaning some whole number times itself. How many of the six faces pass that test?
Only \(1\) and \(4\) are perfect squares, so you have \(2\) favorable faces out of \(6\) total, which gives the fraction \(\frac{2}{6}\). All that is left is to reduce it to lowest terms.
blocks.sol: Start with the total count, since the die is fair every face is equally likely, so there are \(6\) equally likely outcomes and that \(6\) goes on the bottom. Next count the favorable outcomes by checking each face for being a perfect square. The perfect squares from \(1\) to \(6\) are \(1\) and \(4\), because \(1 = 1 \times 1\) and \(4 = 2 \times 2\), while \(2, 3, 5,\) and \(6\) are not. So \(2\) of the \(6\) faces work, and the probability is the favorable count over the total count, which gives \(\frac{2}{6}\). Reducing that fraction by dividing top and bottom by \(2\) gives \(\frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}\). $$\boxed{\frac{1}{3}}$$

blocks.x: You roll a fair \(6\)-sided die one time. Its faces are the numbers \(1, 2, 3, 4, 5,\) and \(6\), and each face is equally likely to land up. Find the probability that the number you roll is one of \(1\) to \(6\). Give your answer as a fraction in lowest terms, unless the event is certain or impossible, in which case answer with the whole number \(1\) or \(0\).
hints: First nail down the equally likely outcomes. A fair die has \(6\) faces that can come up, and each is just as likely as the others, so your total count is \(6\).

Now count the favorable outcomes, the rolls that count as a win. Every single face shows a number from \(1\) to \(6\), so ask yourself how many of the \(6\) faces fit, and notice that none of them are left out.
All \(6\) faces are favorable, so the probability is \(\frac{6}{6}\). Simplify that fraction and think about what it means when a win is guaranteed.
blocks.sol: Start by counting the total. A fair die has \(6\) equally likely faces, so the total count is \(6\). Now count the favorable outcomes, the ones that make the event happen. The event is rolling a number from \(1\) to \(6\), and every one of the \(6\) faces is between \(1\) and \(6\), so all \(6\) outcomes are favorable. That gives a favorable count of \(6\) over a total count of \(6\), which is \(\frac{6}{6} = 1\). This makes sense, because no matter what you roll you are guaranteed to land on a number from \(1\) to \(6\), so the event is certain. A certain event has probability \(1\). $$\boxed{1}$$

blocks.x: A fair \(6\)-sided die is rolled once. Its faces are numbered \(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\), and each face is equally likely to land up. Find the probability of rolling a \(7\). Give your answer as a fraction in lowest terms, and if the event is certain or impossible give the whole number \(1\) or \(0\).
hints: First settle what is equally likely. A fair die has faces numbered \(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\), and every one of those faces is equally likely to land up, so there are \(6\) equally likely outcomes in all. That \(6\) is your total count for the bottom of the fraction.

Now look for the favorable outcomes, the ones where the die shows a \(7\). Go through the faces \(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\) and check whether any of them is a \(7\). How many of the \(6\) faces actually show a \(7\)?
None of the faces is a \(7\), so the favorable count is \(0\). Put that favorable count over the total of \(6\) and simplify \(\frac{0}{6}\).
blocks.sol:
Probability is just careful counting wearing a new coat, so let us start by counting what is equally likely. A fair die has faces numbered \(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\), and each face is equally likely to land up, which gives a total count of \(6\) equally likely outcomes.
\nNow count the favorable outcomes, the faces that show a \(7\). Looking through \(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\), none of the \(6\) faces is a \(7\), so the favorable count is \(0\).
\nPut the favorable count over the total count and you get $$\frac{0}{6} = 0.$$
\nThere is simply no way for a die numbered \(1\) through \(6\) to show a \(7\), so this is an impossible event, and an impossible event sits at the very start of the number line with probability \(\boxed{0}\).

blocks.x: KEY IDEA, the probability scale. A probability is a part of the whole, so it can never dip below \(0\) or climb above \(1\), and it always lands somewhere from \(0\) to \(1\). Something impossible sits right at \(0\), something certain sits right at \(1\), and everything else lives in between. The closer a probability sits to \(1\), the more likely the event, and the closer it sits to \(0\), the less likely it is.

<svg viewBox="0 0 420 150" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="A probability scale drawn as a number line from 0 on the left to 1 on the right. The left end at 0 is labelled impossible, the middle at one half is labelled even chance, and the right end at 1 is labelled certain. An arrow shows that moving right means more likely."><text x="210.0" y="26" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="var(--mid)">EVERY PROBABILITY LIVES BETWEEN 0 AND 1</text><line x1="50" y1="70" x2="370" y2="70" stroke="var(--slate)" stroke-width="3"/><line x1="50.0" y1="61" x2="50.0" y2="79" stroke="var(--ink)" stroke-width="3"/><circle cx="50.0" cy="70" r="6" fill="var(--gold2)" stroke="var(--gold-deep)" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="50.0" y="52" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="15" font-weight="700" fill="var(--ink)">0</text><text x="50.0" y="100" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--slate)">impossible</text><line x1="210.0" y1="61" x2="210.0" y2="79" stroke="var(--ink)" stroke-width="3"/><circle cx="210.0" cy="70" r="6" fill="var(--gold2)" stroke="var(--gold-deep)" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210.0" y="52" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="15" font-weight="700" fill="var(--ink)">½</text><text x="210.0" y="100" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--slate)">even chance</text><line x1="370.0" y1="61" x2="370.0" y2="79" stroke="var(--ink)" stroke-width="3"/><circle cx="370.0" cy="70" r="6" fill="var(--gold2)" stroke="var(--gold-deep)" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="370.0" y="52" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="15" font-weight="700" fill="var(--ink)">1</text><text x="370.0" y="100" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="var(--slate)">certain</text><text x="210.0" y="128" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="11" font-style="italic" fill="var(--mid)">more likely →</text></svg>
blocks.cap: Reading left to right, every probability is just a fraction of the whole, so it always lands somewhere on this line between \(0\) and \(1\). At the far left sits \(0\), which means impossible, and at the far right sits \(1\), which means certain. Right in the middle, \(\frac{1}{2}\) is an even chance, and the further right you go the more likely the event becomes.

blocks.x: KEY IDEA, the equally-likely warning. Favorable over total only tells the truth when the outcomes you count are all EQUALLY LIKELY, so before you count anything, ask what the truly equal outcomes are. On a die with colored sides the equal outcomes are the \(6\) sides, not the colors, because the colors might be split unevenly. So count the sides. If \(2\) of the \(6\) sides are painted red, the chance of red is \(\frac{2}{6}\), even though red is only \(1\) color out of however many. Whenever something feels uneven, dig down to the layer where every outcome really does have the same chance, and count there.

<svg viewBox="0 0 420 150" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="A bar split into 5 equal regions of the same size, so each region is equally likely. 3 of the regions are shaded gold and counted as favorable, and the other 2 are left plain. The probability of landing on a shaded region is 3 out of 5, which is 3 fifths."><text x="210.0" y="26" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="var(--mid)">COUNT THE EQUAL REGIONS</text><rect x="50" y="46" width="64" height="64" fill="var(--gold2)" stroke="var(--slate)" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="82.0" y="83.0" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="14" font-weight="700" fill="var(--gold-deep)">★</text><rect x="114" y="46" width="64" height="64" fill="var(--gold2)" stroke="var(--slate)" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="146.0" y="83.0" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="14" font-weight="700" fill="var(--gold-deep)">★</text><rect x="178" y="46" width="64" height="64" fill="var(--gold2)" stroke="var(--slate)" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210.0" y="83.0" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="14" font-weight="700" fill="var(--gold-deep)">★</text><rect x="242" y="46" width="64" height="64" fill="var(--softer)" stroke="var(--slate)" stroke-width="1.5"/><rect x="306" y="46" width="64" height="64" fill="var(--softer)" stroke="var(--slate)" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210.0" y="140" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="700" fill="var(--ink)">favorable 3  ÷  total 5  =  3/5</text></svg>
blocks.cap: Look at the bar above. It is split into \(5\) regions that are all the same size, and \(3\) of them are shaded. Because every region is exactly as big as the others, landing on any one of them is equally likely, so we can just count. There are \(3\) shaded regions we want out of \(5\) regions in total, which gives a probability of \(\frac{3}{5}\). This only works because the regions are equal in size. If the pieces were different sizes they would not be equally likely, and counting them like this would no longer tell you the chance.

blocks.x: A special \(8\)-sided die has \(2\) red sides, \(5\) yellow sides, and \(1\) blue side, and every side is equally likely to land face up. You give it one roll. What is the probability of rolling a yellow side? Give your answer as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: Probability is a counting question wearing a new coat. The equally likely outcomes are the sides of the die, not the colors, so first ask how many sides there are in all. There are \(8\) of them.
Now count only the outcomes you are hoping for. A roll counts as a win when the side that lands up is yellow, and there are \(5\) yellow sides, so \(5\) of the \(8\) sides are favorable.
Put the favorable count over the total count, which gives \(\frac{5}{8}\). Check whether that fraction can be made any simpler.
blocks.sol: Count the sides, not the colors. The die has \(8\) sides, and since every side is equally likely to land up, those \(8\) sides are the equally likely outcomes, so the total count is \(8\). Now count the favorable outcomes, the ones where a yellow side comes up. There are \(5\) yellow sides, so the favorable count is \(5\). The probability is the favorable count over the total count, which gives $$\frac{5}{8}.$$ This fraction is already in lowest terms, since \(5\) and \(8\) share no common factor bigger than \(1\). So the probability of rolling yellow is \(\frac{5}{8}\).

blocks.x: A standard deck has \(52\) cards split evenly into \(4\) suits, the spades, the hearts, the diamonds, and the clubs, with \(13\) cards in each suit. The deck is shuffled really well, so the top card is equally likely to be any one of the \(52\) cards. You flip over the top card and hope for a spade. What is the probability that the top card is a spade? Give your answer as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: Every card on top is equally likely, so the total number of equally likely outcomes is just the number of cards in the whole deck, which is \(52\).
Now count the favorable outcomes, the ones you are rooting for. A spade is what counts here, and there are \(13\) spades in the deck.
Put the favorable count over the total count to get \(\frac{13}{52}\), then simplify that fraction all the way down.
blocks.sol: Start by deciding what is equally likely. The deck was shuffled well, so the top card has the same chance of being any one of the \(52\) cards, and that gives you \(52\) equally likely outcomes for the total. Next count the favorable outcomes, the ones that make you happy. You want a spade, and a full suit has \(13\) cards, so there are \(13\) spades. The probability is the favorable count over the total count, which gives \(\frac{13}{52}\). Now simplify, and since \(13\) divides into \(52\) exactly four times you get $$\frac{13}{52} = \frac{1}{4}.$$ That matches what you would expect, because the spades are one of the four equal suits, so they take up exactly one quarter of the deck. $$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{4}}$$

blocks.x: A standard \(52\)-card deck is shuffled well and placed face down. You flip over the top card. What is the probability that the top card is a \(9\)? Give your answer as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: Since the deck was shuffled well, every one of the \(52\) cards is equally likely to be the top card. So your total count is \(52\), one for each card that could be sitting on top.
Now count the favorable cards, the ones that make the top card a \(9\). A standard deck has one \(9\) in each of the four suits, so think about how many \(9\)s there are in all.
You have \(4\) favorable cards out of \(52\) total, so the probability is \(\frac{4}{52}\). All that is left is to reduce that fraction to lowest terms.
blocks.sol:
Because the deck was shuffled well, every card is just as likely as any other to land on top, so all \(52\) cards are equally likely. That means the total count is \(52\).
\nNow we count the favorable outcomes, the cards that would make the top card a \(9\). There are \(4\) nines, one in each suit, so the favorable count is \(4\).
\nThe probability is the favorable count over the total count, which gives $$\frac{4}{52} = \frac{1}{13}.$$
\nSo the chance that the top card is a \(9\) is \(\boxed{\frac{1}{13}}\).

blocks.x: A standard \(52\)-card deck is shuffled, and a face card means a Jack, a Queen, or a King. Find the probability that the top card is a face card. Give your answer as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: Every card in the shuffled deck is equally likely to land on top, so the total number of equally likely outcomes is just the number of cards in the deck, which is \(52\).

Now count the favorable cards, the ones that make the top card a face card. A face card is a Jack, a Queen, or a King, so there are \(3\) face ranks, and each rank shows up once in every suit. Count how many face cards there are in the whole deck.
There are \(3\) face ranks in each of \(4\) suits, so \(3 \times 4 = 12\) favorable cards out of \(52\) total. All that is left is to write \(\frac{12}{52}\) and reduce it to lowest terms.
blocks.sol:
Since the deck is shuffled, every one of the \(52\) cards is equally likely to end up on top, so the total count is \(52\).
\nNow we count the favorable cards. A face card is a Jack, a Queen, or a King, which is \(3\) face ranks, and each of those ranks appears once in every suit. There are \(4\) suits, so the number of face cards is $$3 \times 4 = 12.$$
\nThat gives \(12\) favorable cards out of \(52\) total, so the probability is \(\frac{12}{52}\). Both numbers share a factor of \(4\), and dividing top and bottom by \(4\) gives $$\frac{12}{52} = \frac{3}{13}.$$
\nSo the probability that the top card is a face card is \(\boxed{\frac{3}{13}}\).

blocks.x: KEY IDEA, two stages multiply. When an experiment happens in stages, the total number of equally likely outcomes does not add, it MULTIPLIES, and that is just the Multiplication Principle from 10.2 showing up again. Two dice are not \(12\) outcomes, they are \(6 \times 6 = 36\), one for every cell of a \(6\) by \(6\) grid. So lay out the grid, count the favorable cells, and put that over \(36\). Multiply to find the total, then count what you want.
